---
title: "Deviance bias under congenial multiple imputation: a sourced derivation"
subtitle: "Canonical formal derivation for the JAIGP theory paper (setup, assumptions, proof)"
author: "mi-spectral"
date: last-modified
bibliography: ../literature/mi-spectral-better-bibTeX.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    grid:
      margin-width: 350px
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  cache: false
---

## Introduction {#sec-intro}

### The problem {#sec-intro-problem}

Multiple imputation (MI) replaces each missing entry with several plausible draws, lets
the analyst apply complete-data tools to each completed data set, and combines the
results [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Many of those complete-data tools —
likelihood-ratio statistics, information criteria, profile likelihoods, deviance-based
fit indices — are built, directly or indirectly, on the **imputation-averaged complete-data
log-likelihood**: the Q-function of the EM and congeniality literatures
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997; @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a].
Write $\bar Q_\infty(\theta)$ for its idealized infinite-imputation limit. One would like
$\bar Q_\infty$ to behave like the complete-data log-likelihood $\ell_{\text{com}}$ that
would have been computed had nothing been missing. It does not. Evaluated at the
observed-data estimator and measured against the complete-data gold standard
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, the Q-function is systematically biased. This
document derives that bias exactly, to leading order, and names the two mechanisms behind it.

### The result {#sec-intro-result}

Let $\mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ denote the matrix
relative increase in variance due to missingness — Rubin's scalar $r$ in matrix form.
Under congenial, proper MI, taking the complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ as the
target, the Q-function deviance bias is
$$
\mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]
= \underbrace{+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{imputation bias}}
\;\;\underbrace{-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{estimation mismatch}}
\;=\; +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}),
$$
to $O(n^{-1})$ (on the deviance scale, double this). The first term is **imputation
bias**: averaging $\ell_{\text{com}}$ over completions drawn from the imputation model
overstates the fit by $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The second is **estimation
mismatch**: MI's estimand is $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, whereas $\bar Q_\infty$ is centered
at the observed-data maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, and the gap between them costs
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The two do not cancel — they leave a net upward
bias of half the trace. This $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ holds **to leading order
under MCAR** (the design-imbalance term is $O(n^{-1})$) for any regular likelihood **whose
conditional missing-data entropy depends on the estimated parameters** — concretely, an estimated
scale or covariance, as in the multivariate normal we instantiate throughout. That dependence is
essential: with a *known* scale (a location-only fit) the conditional entropy is parameter-free and
the net bias collapses to $\mathbb{E}[T]=0$ under FIML and $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
under proper MI (scope remark after the theorem). **Under MAR** Term A carries one further $O(1)$ piece — a
**design-imbalance** term $(A)+(C)$, nonzero exactly when the missing and observed units have
different conditioning-variable distributions and zero under MCAR — developed in @sec-termA.

### Why it matters {#sec-intro-why}

Whatever is built on $\bar Q_\infty$ inherits this bias. Two applications motivate the
paper; both are developed in the manuscript, not here.

- **Likelihood-ratio model comparison.** The MI likelihood-ratio numerator
$\hat d_L = 2[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*) - \bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi_0^*)]$ — where $\hat\psi^*$
maximizes the averaged log-likelihood, in the sense of
[@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022] — inherits a *differential* bias
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi^*)) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}(\hat\psi_0^*))$.
It vanishes under the null but not under local alternatives or non-nested comparison,
distorting power and ranking even when the reference distribution is correctly calibrated
[@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022].
- **Information criteria.** MI-AIC and MI-BIC are built from $-2\bar Q_\infty$, so each
candidate model carries a bias $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ that is *model-specific* —
RIV depends on the model. Left uncorrected, the criteria systematically favor higher-RIV
models; adding $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ to the deviance restores complete-data-equivalent
selection. The corrected criterion **coincides with the missing-data information criterion
$\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ of [@shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017]**, whose penalty is *half* that
of the earlier complete-data-discrepancy criteria — AICcd
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] (and the related PDIO) carry
$2\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and over-penalize, while the missing-covariate AIC of
[@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008] targets a different (adjusted-likelihood)
discrepancy. What is new here is not that penalty but its imputation/estimation decomposition, the
MAR design-imbalance correction $(A)+(C)$, and the extension to proper MI (@sec-intro-lineage).

### Relation to existing work {#sec-intro-lineage}

The result sits *upstream* of the MI test-calibration literature. Meng and Rubin's
combining rule for likelihood-ratio tests [@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b], its
refinement by Chan and Meng [@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022], and Chan's general
stacked-MI tests [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] all concern how to *calibrate the
reference distribution* of an MI test statistic, taking the statistic's numerator as
given. We address the *bias of that numerator*. The two are complementary: calibration
makes the reference distribution right; the present correction makes the statistic
referred to it centered. On the model-selection side, the complete-data-discrepancy criteria
PDIO and AICcd [@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a] attach a $2\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
missing-data penalty to the complete-data deviance under EM-based estimation;
[@shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017] showed this *doubles* the correct value and derived
$\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ with the *halved* penalty $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. Our net correction
reproduces exactly that $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — so as a penalty the headline IC result is
**not new: it is $\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$.** What *is* new is threefold: (i) the decomposition of that
penalty into an imputation-bias term $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and an estimation-mismatch
term $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (MI's target is $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, not the EM
pivot $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$); (ii) the **MAR design-imbalance correction** $(A)+(C)$, which lies
beyond the i.i.d. fixed-pattern setting of [@shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017] — named
there (§8) as future work in its concluding remarks ("combine a missing mechanism with other
sampling mechanisms, such as the covariate-shift ... problem"), the covariate-shift machinery
being [@shimodairahidetoshiImprovingPredictiveInference2000]; and (iii) the extension from the
deterministic EM Q-function to **proper multiple imputation**. Claeskens and Consentino's
missing-covariate AIC [@claeskensconsentinoVariableSelectionIncomplete2008] targets a different
(EM-adjusted-likelihood) discrepancy with a Takeuchi-type penalty, and so sits outside this line.
A third route — Hens, Aerts and Molenberghs's weighted AIC
[@hensModelSelectionIncomplete2006a] — reaches the same complete-data-equivalent target by
inverse-probability (Horvitz–Thompson) reweighting of the complete cases rather than a deviance
correction: it needs estimated selection probabilities, keeps the penalty at $2q$, and explicitly
leaves an *imputation*-based criterion — which the present $(A)+(C)$ term bias-corrects —
as open. The design-imbalance bias it diagnoses (its complete-case discrepancy shifts by
$\operatorname{Cov}(y,\pi)/\mathbb{E}[\pi]$ under MAR) is the same phenomenon $(A)+(C)$ captures
intrinsically within MI.

### Road map {#sec-intro-roadmap}

@sec-notation fixes notation: the model and missingness, the two likelihoods and their
maximizers, the three Fisher informations and the identity binding them, RIV, the
Q-function, and the bias decomposition $T = A + B$. @sec-ledger collects the regularity,
congeniality, and properness conditions together with the asymptotic lemmas the proof
rests on. @sec-derivation states the theorem, sketches the proof strategy, and gives the
proof: Term B (estimation mismatch) is an elementary Taylor argument, while Term A
(imputation bias) is a second-order expansion turning on a cancellation we isolate as a
lemma. @sec-sources reproduces the cited source passages verbatim, and @sec-verify
verifies the trace identities symbolically and the normal instantiation numerically,
executed inline at render time.

A note on provenance. Because this is an AI-assisted derivation, every external claim is
sourced. The main column carries the mathematics; the right margin carries the provenance
— the source's own equation or theorem number, the reproduced statement, and a line
reference into the local `literature/` copy (with a short verbatim snippet, so the pointer
survives re-pagination). Steps proved here rather than cited are flagged `[derived]`;
those additionally checked symbolically in @sec-verify are flagged `[mc]`. Independently,
the pre-registered Monte Carlo study in `verification/` estimates
$\mathbb{E}[A],\mathbb{E}[B],\mathbb{E}[T]$ across configurations and compares them with the
analytic $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

---

## Setup and notation {#sec-notation}

This section fixes the objects the theorem is about: the data and model, the two
likelihoods and their maximizers, the three Fisher informations and the identity binding
them, the relative increase in variance, the Q-function, and the bias decomposition
$T = A + B$. The conditions and asymptotic results the proof *rests on* — regularity,
congeniality, properness, and the expansions — are collected separately in @sec-ledger.
Definitions carry a label (D1, D2, …) so that the margin provenance and the later proof
can point back to them.

### Data, model, and missingness {#sec-data}

**D1 (data and model).** Let $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times p}$ collect $N$ complete
observations, the rows drawn independently from a $p$-variate normal,
$Y_i \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu,\Sigma)$ with $\Sigma \succ 0$. The estimand is the
mean–covariance parameter $\theta = (\mu, \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$, of dimension
$k = p + \tfrac12 p(p+1)$. Missingness, introduced next, will keep us from observing $Y$
in full.

::: {.column-margin}
**Convention.** Complete data $Y=(Y_{\text{obs}},Y_{\text{mis}})$ follows
@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1477</code></a>, where $\ell(\theta\mid Y)$,
$\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})$, $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$ appear
and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:438</code></a>. Meng's congeniality framework writes
$Z_c=\{X,Y_{\text{inc}},I\}$, $Z_o=\{X,Y_{\text{obs}},I,R_{\text{inc}}\}$ with $X$ =
always-observed covariates [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a], <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:95</code></a>
— a different $X$, which is why we avoid the symbol. The verification code
(`verification/00-setup.R`) labels the realized complete matrix `X`; that is our $Y$.
:::

**D2 (missingness).** A response indicator $R$ (with $1$ marking a missing entry) splits
each row into its observed and missing parts, $Y=(Y_{\text{obs}},Y_{\text{mis}})$.
Throughout we assume the missingness is ignorable — missing at random, with the
missingness parameters distinct from $\theta$ — so the mechanism itself need not be
modeled. The precise conditions are stated as assumptions in @sec-ledger.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinInferenceMissingData1976b, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1976"><code>:39</code></a> and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:3263</code></a>: ignorability (MAR + parameter distinctness).
:::

### Likelihoods and estimators {#sec-likelihoods}

Missingness forces a distinction the whole argument turns on: the likelihood we *would*
have maximized with complete data versus the one we *can* maximize from what is observed,
and their two different maximizers.

**D3 (complete-data log-likelihood and MLE).** Had $Y$ been observed in full, we would form
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y) = \sum_i \log f_{\mathcal{N}}(Y_i; \mu,\Sigma)$ and maximize it
at the complete-data MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{com}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell_{\text{com}}(\theta; Y)$.
This is the gold standard MI aims to recover.

**D4 (observed-data log-likelihood and MLE).** From the observed entries alone, each row
contributes only its marginal density, giving
$\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta; Y_{\text{obs}}) = \sum_i \log f_{\mathcal{N}}(Y_{i,\text{obs}}; \mu_{O_i},\Sigma_{O_iO_i})$,
maximized at the observed-data (FIML) MLE $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell_{\text{obs}}$.
The gap between $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ and $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$ is the source of Term B.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1510</code></a>: the observed-data MLE is the maximizer of $\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})$ — "$\hat\theta$ is now the observed-data MLE, the maximizer of $l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})$".
:::

### Information matrices {#sec-information}

Each estimator's precision is governed by a Fisher information, and the two informations
differ by exactly the information the missing data would have supplied.

**D5 (complete information).** The complete-data Fisher information is
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = -\,\mathbb{E}_{Y\sim\theta}\!\big[\partial^2\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top\big] = \mathbb{E}\big[S_{\text{com}}(\theta)\,S_{\text{com}}(\theta)^\top\big]. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>: Fisher information $I_\theta=\mathbb{E}_\theta[\dot\ell_\theta\dot\ell_\theta^\top]$ and curvature $P_\theta\ddot\ell_\theta=-I_\theta$. MVN closed form: D15.
:::

**D6 (observed information).** The observed-data (expected) Fisher information — the
asymptotic precision of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ (Lemma L1) — is
$$ I_{\text{obs}}(\theta) = -\,\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}\sim\theta}\!\big[\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y_{\text{obs}})/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top\big]. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1489</code></a>: observed information $I_o=-\partial^2\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})/\partial\theta^2$; we use its expectation, the Fisher form (@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>), taken **unconditionally** over $(Y,R)$ — see @sec-riv. For MVN the naive (MCAR) form is the pattern-mixture sum (`fisher_info_obs_mvn`); under MAR $I_{\text{obs}}$ carries an extra mean–covariance cross term (Appendix C) and is estimated by the observed information.
:::

**D7 (missing information and the information identity).** Their difference is the missing
information, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta) := I_{\text{com}}(\theta) - I_{\text{obs}}(\theta)$,
equivalently the additive identity
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = I_{\text{obs}}(\theta) + I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta). $$ {#eq-info-identity}
It is positive semidefinite, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$: missingness can only
cost information, never create it.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, missing-information principle: Schafer establishes $I_c=I_o+I_m$ *conditional on* $Y_{\text{obs}}$ via the EM decomposition $Q(\theta\mid\phi)=\ell_{\text{obs}}+H(\theta\mid\phi)$, with $I_c=-\partial^2 Q(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1483</code></a>], $I_m=-\partial^2 H(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1495</code></a>], eq (3.19) [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1501</code></a>]. Taking $\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}}$ gives the identity for the Fisher informations D5-D6 (his conditional $I_c,I_o,I_m$ average to our $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}},I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$). $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$ `[mc]` (min eigenvalue $\approx 0$ on the fixture, @lst-mc-psd).
:::

### The relative increase in variance (RIV) {#sec-riv}

The ratio of missing to observed information is the quantity in which the whole result is
written.

**D8 (relative increase in variance).** The matrix relative increase in variance,
$$ \mathrm{RIV}(\theta) := I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\, I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}, $$ {#eq-riv}
is Rubin's scalar $r$ in matrix form — a measure of how much missingness inflates the
variance of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ relative to the complete-data optimum. Its trace is
the scalar that drives the theorem.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source (name, scalar form).** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2194</code></a>: the relative increase in variance due to nonresponse $r_m = (1+m^{-1})B_m/\bar U_m$; with within $\bar U_m=\sum_l U_{*l}/m$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2167</code></a>], between $B_m=\sum_l(\hat Q_{*l}-\bar Q_m)^{t}(\hat Q_{*l}-\bar Q_m)/(m-1)$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2173</code></a>], total $T_m=\bar U_m+(1+m^{-1})B_m$ [<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2179</code></a>].
:::

An equivalent form, used throughout: substituting $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=I_{\text{com}}-I_{\text{obs}}$
(D7) and using $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{obs}}=I_k$,
$$ \mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}} - I_k, \qquad \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k, $$ {#eq-trriv}
so the trace appearing in the theorem is $\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Trace algebra.** @petersen2008matrix, §1.1: trace linearity $\operatorname{Tr}(A+B)=\operatorname{Tr}A+\operatorname{Tr}B$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:183</code></a>], $\operatorname{tr}(I_k)=k$ from $\operatorname{Tr}(A)=\sum_i A_{ii}$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:179</code></a>], and cyclicity $\operatorname{Tr}(AB)=\operatorname{Tr}(BA)$ [<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:182</code></a>].
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Identification: RIV equals Rubin's $r_\infty$
Under congenial proper MI as $M\to\infty$: $\bar U_\infty = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$
(complete-data MLE variance) and $T_\infty = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (variance recovery;
Lemma L4), so $B_\infty = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ and
$$ r_\infty = B_\infty \bar U_\infty^{-1} = (I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1})I_{\text{com}} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}} - I_k = \mathrm{RIV}. $$
Confirmed numerically in @lst-mc-fisher.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Disambiguation — RIV is not the EM rate / "fraction of missing information"

Schafer's **fraction of missing information / EM rate matrix** is
$D = I_{\text{com}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$
[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997 eq (3.21), $D = I_c^{-1}(\hat\theta)I_m(\hat\theta)$], <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1517</code></a>.
It shares $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ with RIV but uses $I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ on the left, not
$I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$. They relate by $D = (I_k+\mathrm{RIV})^{-1}\mathrm{RIV}$ (scalar:
$\gamma = r/(1+r)$). Our headline $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}})-k$
lives specifically on the $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ side; a reader who substitutes $D$ gets the wrong quantity.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## The information behind the RIV: use the observed-data information {#sec-two-rivs}

The RIV's denominator is the observed-data information $I_{\text{obs}}=-\mathbb{E}[\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}]$
(D6), and one subtlety decides what it equals under MAR. The expectation must be taken
**unconditionally** — over the joint law of $(Y,R)$, with the missingness mechanism included —
because under MAR the response pattern $R$ is *not* ancillary for $\theta$
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §2], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:89</code></a>. Taking the
expectation as if each pattern's observed block were a fresh marginal sample — the **naive**
expectation, valid only under MCAR — gives a different, biased information: "the expected
information ... is wrong because the expectation is taken under MCAR"
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:60</code></a>.

For the MVN the difference is explicit. The naive expectation makes $I_{\text{obs}}$ block-diagonal
in $(\mu,\Sigma)$ — the familiar mean/covariance orthogonality — and is exactly what
`fisher_info_obs_mvn` computes. Under MAR, selection on the observed values shifts each pattern's
observed mean off the marginal, and the unconditional information picks up a non-zero
mean–covariance **cross term**, so "the orthogonality of mean and variance-covariance parameters
is lost under the MAR mechanism," vanishing iff the mechanism is MCAR
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §3.2], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:374</code></a> (Appendix C
gives the cross term in closed form). Because the unconditional information needs the
usually-unknown mechanism, the standing recommendation — which we adopt — is to estimate
$I_{\text{obs}}$ by the **observed** information $-\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}/\partial\theta\partial\theta^\top$,
which is consistent and needs no mechanism model: "provided the observed information matrix is
used, conventional likelihood based frequentist inference is applicable in the MAR setting"
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §5], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:544</code></a>. So there is a
**single** RIV, $\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})$, evaluated with
the observed-data information; both deviance-bias terms calibrate to it (note after @eq-combine).

**A separate axis — score variance vs curvature — does not bite here.** The expected (curvature)
information $-\mathbb{E}[\partial^2\ell]$ and the score variance $\mathbb{E}[SS^\top]$ coincide
under correct specification — the second Bartlett identity, or *information equality* — which is
why D5 writes $I_{\text{com}}=-\mathbb{E}[\partial^2\ell]=\mathbb{E}[S_{\text{com}}S_{\text{com}}^\top]$
as one object [@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013, §13.6: "assuming the model is correct we get
$\mathcal{J}=\mathcal{I}$, and the formula reduces to ... the 'naive' variance"], <a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14699</code></a>.
Their inequality *under misspecification* is what turns AIC's penalty $k$ into Takeuchi's
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ (TIC/GIC)
[@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013; @konishiGeneralisedInformationCriteria1996], <a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14707</code></a>, <a href="#sec-src-konishi"><code>:90</code></a>
— a regime that is **not** ours: the analysis model is correctly specified, so information
equality holds, confirmed numerically with $\operatorname{tr}(\hat{\mathcal{J}}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})\approx k$
across cells (`run_all.R` phase 6, `verification/cache/run_all-prod/phase6-info/`). That axis is
about misspecification; the MAR distinction above is about *which sampling distribution* the
expectation is taken over, and it is the one that matters here.

**Symbol caution.** The proof below writes $\mathcal{J}$ for the *observed information* (the
negative Hessian $-\partial^2\ell/\partial\theta\partial\theta^\top$ at the estimate), the
EM/Louis convention. This is **not** Pawitan's $\mathcal{J}$ (the score variance
$\mathbb{E}[SS^\top]$); under correct specification both reduce to $I$, so the body's
$\mathcal{J}\to_p I$ steps are unaffected.
:::

### The MI Q-function and the deviance-bias split {#sec-qfun}

We can now name the object the theorem is about, and the bias attributed to it.

**D9 (MI Q-function).** Multiple imputation does not maximize $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ directly; it
averages the complete-data log-likelihood over completions drawn from the analyst's *fitted*
imputation model. Write $\tilde\phi$ for the imputation parameter — under congenial proper MI a
draw from the imputation posterior fit to $Y_{\text{obs}}$, centered at $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$
(the symbol $\phi$ rather than $\theta$ marks it as the imputer's; see @sec-derivation). Each
completion is $\tilde Y_{\text{mis}}^{(l)} \sim P(\,\cdot \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, \tilde\phi^{(l)})$,
and in the infinite-imputation limit
$$ \bar Q_\infty(\theta) := \lim_{M\to\infty}\frac1M\sum_{l=1}^M \ell_{\text{com}}\big(\theta; Y_{\text{obs}}, \tilde Y_{\text{mis}}^{(l)}\big) = \mathbb{E}_{\tilde\phi}\,\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\,\tilde\phi}\!\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)\big]. $$
This is the EM/Meng Q-function $Q(\theta\mid\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ in the proper-MI limit: the
inner expectation conditions on the *fitted* $\tilde\phi\approx\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, exactly as
the EM E-step conditions $Q(\theta\mid\theta^{(t)})$ on the current iterate — **not** on the
unknown truth $\theta_0$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** Deterministic-FIML evaluation `barQ_fiml_analytic`$(\cdot,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$
(`verification/00-setup.R`) conditions the completion at the fitted $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$;
`[mc]` equal to this construction on the fixture (@lst-mc-fiml). The fitted-vs-oracle distinction
(callout below) is checked in `verification/rederivation-L0-fitted-vs-oracle.R`.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## $\bar Q_\infty$ is the *fitted* predictive, not the oracle conditional expectation

It is tempting to write $\bar Q_\infty(\theta)=\mathbb{E}[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$,
the genuine conditional expectation under the data-generating law. That is **wrong**, and the slip
is not cosmetic: it flips the sign of the headline. The genuine conditional expectation averages
$Y_{\text{mis}}$ over the *true* law $P(\cdot\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta_0)$; $\bar Q_\infty$ averages
over the *fitted* imputation law $P(\cdot\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\tilde\phi)$ with
$\tilde\phi\approx\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$. The two agree only if $\tilde\phi=\theta_0$, which it is
not — the analyst imputes from a model fit to the data, not from an oracle. The gap between them
**is** the imputation bias Term A (@sec-termA), $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.

The distinction is decisive because $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ is a function of $Y_{\text{obs}}$
alone. Were $\bar Q_\infty$ the oracle conditional expectation, iterated expectation (the tower
property) would give
$$ \mathbb{E}\big[\,\mathbb{E}[\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\mid Y_{\text{obs}}] - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\,\big] = 0, $$
so Term A would vanish and the deviance bias would collapse to $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.
Term A is instead $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ precisely because the completions are drawn at
the fitted $\tilde\phi$, not at $\theta_0$. This is confirmed directly: imputing from the true
$\theta_0$ drives $\mathbb{E}[A]\to 0$ and the total to $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$,
while imputing from the fitted $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ — deterministic FIML and proper MI alike —
gives $\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and the total $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
(`verification/rederivation-L0-fitted-vs-oracle.R`).
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Notation clash with Rubin's pooled estimand

$\bar Q_\infty$ here is the averaged *log-likelihood*. It is **not** Rubin's
pooled point estimate $\bar Q_m = \sum_l \hat Q_{*l}/m$
[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987], <a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:2161</code></a>, which averages complete-data
*estimates*. We reserve $\bar Q_\infty$ for the averaged log-likelihood throughout.
:::

**D10–D12 (deviance-bias terms).** The theorem compares $\bar Q_\infty$ at
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ against the complete-data gold standard
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})$, split at the pivot $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ into an
imputation-bias term $A$ and an estimation-mismatch term $B$ (on the log-likelihood scale;
the deviance scale doubles each):
$$
A := \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})
\quad(\text{imputation bias}),
$$
$$
B := \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})
\quad(\text{estimation mismatch}),
$$
$$
T := A + B = \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})
\quad(\text{total deviance bias}).
$$ {#eq-decomp}

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** This work; operationalized in the Monte Carlo study (`verification/`).
The theorem (@sec-derivation): $\mathbb{E}[T]=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$,
$\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.
:::

### Congeniality {#sec-congeniality}

**D13 (congeniality).** We assume the imputation and analysis models are congenial in
Meng's sense — loosely, the imputer's posterior moments agree with the analyst's
procedure. This is what licenses the variance-recovery and Q-function identities the proof
relies on (Lemma L4); it is stated precisely as an assumption in @sec-ledger.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Definitions 1-2: posterior moments match the analyst's procedure — $[\hat\theta(Z_o),U(Z_o)]\simeq[E_f(\theta\mid Z_o),V_f(\theta\mid Z_o)]$ [eq (2.3.1), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:103</code></a>] and $[\hat\theta(Z_c),U(Z_c)]\simeq[E_f(\theta\mid Z_c),V_f(\theta\mid Z_c)]$ [eq (2.3.2), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:109</code></a>]; Definition 2 adds $f(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Z_o)=g(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Z_o,A)$ [eq (2.3.3), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:121</code></a>].
:::

### vech and the duplication matrix {#sec-vech}

Because $\theta$ carries $\Sigma$ through $\operatorname{vech}\Sigma$, the normal information
matrices below are written with the duplication matrix relating the half- and
full-vectorizations of a symmetric matrix.

**D14 (half-vectorization and duplication matrix).** For symmetric $A$ ($n\times n$),
$\operatorname{vech}(A)$ stacks the $\tfrac12 n(n+1)$ entries of its lower triangle, and the
duplication matrix $D_n$ satisfies
$$ D_n \operatorname{vech}(A) = \operatorname{vec} A, \qquad \operatorname{vech}(A) = (D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'\operatorname{vec} A. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019, Ch 3 §15: duplication matrix $D_n\operatorname{vech}(A)=\operatorname{vec}A$ (eq (23)) [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1658</code></a>], $\operatorname{vech}$ definition [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1652</code></a>], inverse identity $(D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n)^{-1}=(D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'(A^{-1}\otimes A^{-1})D_n(D_n'D_n)^{-1}$ [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1692</code></a>].
:::

### MVN instantiation of the information matrices {#sec-mvn-info}

For the normal model the complete information has a closed form — the one the verification
in @sec-verify computes with.

**D15 (closed-form MVN information).** On $\theta=(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ the
complete information is block-diagonal,
$$ I_{\text{com}}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} N\Sigma^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{N}{2}D_p'(\Sigma^{-1}\otimes\Sigma^{-1})D_p \end{pmatrix}. $$

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019, MVN ML section: information for $\mu$ and $\operatorname{vech}(\Omega)$ as $n\Omega^{-1}$ and $\tfrac{n}{2}D_m'(\Omega^{-1}\otimes\Omega^{-1})D_m$ [<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1800</code></a>-<a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1813</code></a>] ($\Omega\!\leftrightarrow\!\Sigma$, $m\!\leftrightarrow\!p$, $n\!\leftrightarrow\!N$). $I_{\text{obs}}$ is the pattern-mixture sum of per-pattern marginal informations (`fisher_info_obs_mvn`, `verification/00-setup.R`); `[mc]` against this closed form in @lst-mc-fisher.
:::

---

## Assumptions and lemmas {#sec-ledger}

This section collects what the proof rests on but does not itself establish: the
regularity, ignorability, congeniality, and properness conditions (R1–R7); the standard
matrix and expectation identities; and four asymptotic lemmas (L1–L4). Each labeled item
is cited by the proof in @sec-derivation. As in @sec-notation, provenance — source,
equation or theorem number, and line reference, with any `[mc]`/`[derived]` status — sits
in the margin.

### Regularity, congeniality, and properness {#sec-reg}

Seven conditions are in force throughout, and each is simply the hypothesis under which
some later step is exact to leading order. R1–R2 are the standard likelihood-asymptotics
regularity that makes the Fisher informations and the MLE expansions of @sec-lem
well-defined. R3 makes the missingness ignorable, so the mechanism drops out of the
likelihood (D2). R4–R7 are the multiple-imputation conditions — congeniality, properness,
self-efficiency, and the infinite-imputation idealization — that let the imputation limit
recover the observed-data variance (Lemma L4) and that fix
$\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ in Term A. None is exotic.

**R1 (regular model).** The complete-data model is differentiable in quadratic mean, with
Lipschitz log-density and a consistent MLE; differentiation and integration interchange, so
$P_\theta\dot\ell_\theta=0$ (scores are mean-zero) and $P_\theta\ddot\ell_\theta=-I_\theta$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, Thm 5.39: regularity conditions [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7388</code></a>] and score identities [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>]; @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, <a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1480</code></a>: "sufficient regularity to interchange differentiation and integration".
:::

**R2 (nonsingular information).** Both informations are positive definite,
$I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}}\succ 0$, so the inverses in RIV and in the variance formulas
below exist.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, §5.5: nonsingular Fisher information $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}}\succ 0$ (see @sec-src-vaart).
:::

**R3 (ignorability).** The data are missing at random, with the missingness parameters
distinct from $\theta$, so the missingness mechanism need not be modeled (D2).

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinInferenceMissingData1976b, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1976"><code>:39</code></a> and @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, <a href="#sec-src-little"><code>:3263</code></a>: ignorability (MAR + distinct parameters).
:::

**R4 (congeniality).** The imputation and analysis models are congenial in Meng's sense
(D13) — the imputer's posterior moments agree with the analyst's procedure.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Defs 1-2: congeniality [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:99</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:118</code></a>].
:::

**R5 (properness).** The imputation is second-moment proper in Rubin's sense: its posterior
draws carry the correct first two moments, so the between-imputation variance behaves as the
combining rules assume.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987 and @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, cond. (c), <a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:506</code></a>: second-moment proper imputation.
:::

**R6 (self-efficiency).** The analyst's estimator is self-efficient: no convex combination
of the observed- and complete-data estimators beats the complete-data one, i.e. no $\lambda$
makes $\operatorname{MSE}(\lambda\hat\theta(W_o)+(1-\lambda)\hat\theta(W_c))<\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta(W_c))$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a, Def 5: self-efficiency [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:450</code></a>], cond. (a) [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:504</code></a>].
:::

**R7 ($M\to\infty$).** Infinite-imputation idealization ($\bar Q_\infty,T_\infty$);
finite-$M$ corrections are Rubin's.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, §4: infinite-imputation idealization (see @sec-src-rubin1987).
:::

### Matrix and expectation identities {#sec-alg}

The proof uses only standard identities, named at the point of use and reproduced verbatim
with line references in @sec-sources:

- **Trace.** Linearity and cyclicity, and $\operatorname{tr} I_k = k$
  [@petersen2008matrix].
- **vec / Kronecker.** $\operatorname{vec}(AXB)=(B^\top\!\otimes A)\operatorname{vec}X$ and
  $\operatorname{Tr}(A^\top B)=\operatorname{vec}(A)^\top\operatorname{vec}(B)$
  [@petersen2008matrix].
- **Symmetry.** The information matrices are symmetric, with $(A^{-1})^\top=(A^\top)^{-1}$.
- **Duplication matrix.** The identities of @sec-vech, including
  $(D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n)^{-1}=(D_n'D_n)^{-1}D_n'(A^{-1}\!\otimes A^{-1})D_n(D_n'D_n)^{-1}$
  [@magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019].
- **Expectation.** Linearity, iterated expectation
  $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}(X\mid Y)]=\mathbb{E}X$, and the $L^2$-projection characterization of
  conditional expectation [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a].
- **Second-order Taylor expansion** [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a].
- **Gaussian conditional (Schur complement).** For the normal Q-function,
  $\Sigma_{11\cdot 2}=\Sigma_{11}-\Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21}$ with conditional
  mean $\mu^{(1)}+\Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}(x^{(2)}-\mu^{(2)})$
  [@andersonIntroductionMultivariateStatistical2003].
- **Quadratic-form / score-covariance.** The quadratic-form identity
  $\mathbb{E}[v^\top M v]=\operatorname{tr}(M\,\mathbb{E}[vv^\top])$
  [@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a]; combined with the $L^2$-projection identity
  $U_{\text{obs}}=\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{com}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$ (L3), it gives the score-covariance
  identity $\operatorname{Cov}(U_{\text{obs}},U_{\text{com}})=\operatorname{Var}(U_{\text{obs}})$.

### Asymptotic lemmas {#sec-lem}

Four facts about the estimators and the infinite-imputation limit supply the asymptotic
inputs the proof draws on.

**L1 (MLE asymptotic linearity and normality).** Under R1-R2,
$$ \sqrt{n}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt n}\textstyle\sum_i I_{\theta_0}^{-1}\dot\ell_{\theta_0}(X_i)+o_P(1)\;\rightsquigarrow\;N(0,I_{\theta_0}^{-1}), $$
hence $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$, $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, Thm 5.39: linear expansion [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7388</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:7393</code></a>], covariance $I_\theta^{-1}$ [<a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:4170</code></a>].
:::

**L3 (Fisher/Louis score identity).** Differentiating the loglik
decomposition $\ell(\theta\mid Y)=\ell(\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}})+\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)+c$
once and taking $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$ (conditional score mean-zero, R1) gives
$U_{\text{obs}}=\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{com}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]$; hence by the score-covariance
identity (@sec-alg),
$\operatorname{Cov}(U_{\text{obs}},U_{\text{com}})=\operatorname{Var}(U_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}$.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, loglik decomposition: $\ell(\theta\mid Y)=\ell_{\text{obs}}+\log P$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:944</code></a>], $Q/H$ [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:951</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:964</code></a>], 2nd-derivative analog (3.19) [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:1501</code></a>]; @vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, <a href="#sec-src-vaart"><code>:2320</code></a>: score-mean-zero.
:::

**L2 (variance of the estimator gap).**
$$ \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}}) = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}. $$ {#eq-varGap}
*Proof.* By L1, $\hat\theta_\bullet-\theta_0\approx I_\bullet^{-1}U_\bullet$; by L3,
$\operatorname{Cov}(U_{\text{obs}},U_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}$, so
$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}},\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{obs}}I_{\text{com}}^{-1}=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$;
then $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}+I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$.
The variance is of the *gap*, not of $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ alone — the distinction that
keeps Term B at $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ rather than a spurious $-\tfrac12 k$.
This identity is exact only to leading order: it rests on
$\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}},\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ (via L3),
which holds asymptotically with an $O(n^{-1})$ remainder. Evaluated with the observed-data
information $I_{\text{obs}}$ (@sec-riv), it gives the single RIV that Term B calibrates to.

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** `[derived]` from L1, L3, and the score-covariance identity (@sec-alg); verified symbolically (Tier B, @lst-mc-tierb).
:::

**L4 (MI variance recovery).** Under R4-R7, $\bar\theta_\infty$ is consistent
and $T_\infty=\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$. (Anchors the D8 RIV
identification.)

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** The equality $T_\infty=\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ is the variance-recovery property of *congenial, proper* MI (R4): under congeniality the analyst's procedure is the observed-data MLE, and Rubin's proper-imputation variance is randomization-valid for the repeated-sampling variance of the complete-data statistic [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987, §4.2, conditions (4.2.1)-(4.2.2) and Result 4.1, <a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:3489</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-rubin1987"><code>:3563</code></a>]; $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ then holds by regularity (L1). @mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a (Lemma 3 [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:486</code></a>]; Main result [<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:503</code></a>/<a href="#sec-src-meng"><code>:511</code></a>]) gives the efficiency *dominance* — MI is at least as efficient as the analyst's incomplete-data estimator — with equality under congeniality.
:::

## Main result and proof {#sec-derivation}

Each step names the assumptions and lemmas of @sec-ledger that it invokes; a margin `[mc]`
marks a step checked symbolically in @sec-verify. Let $\theta_0$ denote the data-generating
parameter and $\tilde\phi$ the imputation parameter — the symbol $\phi$ rather than
$\theta$ marks it as the imputer's, distinct from the analysis parameter even where the two
spaces coincide under congeniality, and the tilde marks a posterior *draw* (which collapses
to the point value $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ under FIML). We write $S$ for the score and
$\mathcal{J}$ for the observed information (the negative Hessian at the estimate) — *not* the
score-variance $\mathcal{J}$ of @pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013 (<a href="#sec-src-pawitan"><code>:14618</code></a>); under correct
specification both reduce to the expected information $I$ (see @sec-riv), so the
$\mathcal{J}\to_p I$ steps below are unaffected.

### Theorem {#sec-thm}

**Theorem (Q-function deviance bias).** Under R1-R7, for a model that **estimates a scale or
covariance** (so the conditional missing-data entropy depends on the estimated parameters — the
multivariate normal with estimated $\Sigma$ throughout; scope remark below), to leading order
$$ \mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}};Y)\big] = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) \;+\; [(A)+(C)] \;+\; O(n^{-1}), $$ {#eq-theorem}
decomposing (D10-D12) as $\mathbb{E}[A]=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+[(A)+(C)]+O(n^{-1})$
(imputation bias) and $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+O(n^{-1})$ (estimation
mismatch). The deviance-scale statement multiplies by 2.

The term $(A)+(C)$ is a **design-imbalance** correction carried by Term A (@sec-termA): it
**vanishes under MCAR** (missing and observed units share a conditioning-variable distribution),
recovering the clean $\mathbb{E}[T]=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ that holds for any
regular likelihood **with an estimated scale or covariance** (the scope remark below; the headline is
not universal — a known-scale, location-only fit gives $\mathbb{E}[T]=0$ under FIML), and is a nonzero
$O(1)$ under **MAR**. Its general-MVN *structure* is derived
in @sec-termA — $(A)+(C)=\alpha^\top\mathbb{E}[\delta]+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal H\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, with the entropy gradient $\alpha$ supported only on
the covariance parameters — and for the bivariate monotone MVN it has the closed form
$$ (A)+(C) = \tfrac{n_{\text{mis}}}{n_{\text{obs}}}\Big[1-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(Q_{\text{mis}}Q_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big)\Big], $$
$Q_{\text{mis}},Q_{\text{obs}}$ the conditioning-variable second-moment matrices of the missing vs
observed units, so $(A)+(C)=0$ iff $Q_{\text{mis}}=Q_{\text{obs}}$ (MCAR). For the general-MVN
**monotone** case a leading-order analytic *approximation* (expected information) gives the
$n$-independent constant $\alpha^\top\mathbb{E}[\delta]+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal H\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})\approx-0.22$
(`verification/term-a-mar-closedform.R`, with the **MCAR control returning $\approx0$**). For
**non-monotone** patterns the observed-data likelihood does not factor; its covariance-MLE bias
$b_\Sigma$ is instead derived from the general second-order (Cox–Snell) bias of the MLE — confirmed for
the bivariate design by two computer-algebra systems and direct Monte-Carlo, and now carried to
**general $p$** including the four-variate phase-8 ampute design ($k{=}14$;
`verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone{,_genp}.py`), where it reduces exactly to the
bivariate value, meets the score/information identities to machine precision, and recovers the
complete-data bias $-\sigma$ on the always-observed block. **The numerical value of $(A)+(C)$ is
settled to the observed-information convention.** A leading-order analytic approximation gives
$\approx-0.22$ for the four-variate design (right sign and $O(1)$ order); the principled value uses the
*realized* (observed) information [@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998], biased otherwise under MAR,
and is the empirically measured remainder $-0.18\to-0.27$ (monotone) and $-0.34\to-0.46$ (non-monotone)
over $n=800,1500$. The naive expected-information convention would instead give $\approx+0.1\to+0.3$,
differing from the realized value by the $O(1)$ **realized-information correction** $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$
($\approx-0.55$ non-monotone, the observed-minus-naive RIV gap), derived in closed form in @sec-termA
(its block structure and $O(1)$ order independently cross-checked off-lineage) — **not** a finite-$n$
artifact and **not** a $b_\Sigma$ shortfall ($b_\Sigma$ is derived and nearly selection-invariant). What
is firm: $(A)+(C)$ is a nonzero $O(1)$ under MAR and
**vanishes under MCAR** (controls $\approx0$ throughout).

Here $\mathrm{RIV}=\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})$ (D8) is
evaluated with the **observed-data** information $I_{\text{obs}}$ — the unconditional expectation
that, under MAR, carries the mean–covariance cross term and is estimated by the observed
information (@sec-riv). Both Term A and Term B calibrate to this single RIV; the design-imbalance
$(A)+(C)$ is *additional* to it (it is the part of Term A beyond $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$,
not a re-grading of the information).

::: {.callout-note}
## Scope: the headline needs an estimated scale

The $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ result is not universal over regular likelihoods. It
requires the conditional missing-data entropy
$\mathcal{E}(\theta)=-\mathbb{E}_\theta[\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)]$ to depend
on the estimated parameters — equivalently, a nonzero entropy gradient $\alpha=-\mathcal{E}'$. For
the multivariate normal this dependence lives entirely in the covariance ($\alpha_\mu=0$,
$\alpha_\Sigma\neq0$; the warning in @sec-termA), so the headline is a statement about models that
**estimate a scale or covariance** — the regime the paper instantiates and applies.

When the scale is *known* and only a location is estimated the result changes. Then $\alpha=0$, so
piece $(A)$ vanishes; but the cross-entropy curvature does not — for a fixed conditional covariance
$V$ a fourth-moment (Isserlis) calculation gives $H_{\phi\phi}=-I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$, so
piece $(C)=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(H_{\phi\phi}I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
**survives uncancelled, even under MCAR**. The imputation bias halves,
$\mathbb{E}[A]=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$,
and with $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ the net bias collapses to
$$ \mathbb{E}[T]_{\text{known scale}}=\begin{cases}0 & \text{deterministic FIML},\\[2pt]-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) & \text{proper MI},\end{cases} $$
the two arms differing by the posterior-draw imputation variance $C_n=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
— so deterministic FIML and proper MI are **not** interchangeable for a known-scale fit.

A known-variance experiment (estimate the mean, $\Sigma$ fixed) confirms every value to Monte-Carlo
error (`verification/rederivation-knownvar.R`, $R=2\times10^5$:
$\mathbb{E}[A_{\text{fiml}}]/\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=1.04$, $\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{fiml}}]\approx0$,
$\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{proper}}]/(-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}))=0.96$,
$C_n/\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=1.000$), against the estimated-covariance baseline
$\mathbb{E}[T]=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (`verification/rederivation-L0-fitted-vs-oracle.R`).
The information-criteria application (@sec-intro-why) compares multivariate-normal models with an
estimated covariance and so sits squarely in the estimated-scale regime; the known-scale collapse
affects only location-only fits with a fixed dispersion, which the applications do not use.
:::

### Proof strategy {#sec-strategy}

The proof pivots on the observed-data estimator $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, splitting the
total bias into the two terms of @eq-decomp,
$$
T = \underbrace{\big[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\big]}_{A:\ \text{imputation bias}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) - \ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big]}_{B:\ \text{estimation mismatch}},
$$
and evaluating each in expectation to leading order.

*Term B* is elementary. Both quantities are complete-data log-likelihoods, so their
difference is a second-order Taylor expansion of $\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its maximizer
$\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$; its expectation is
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)$.
The variance of the estimator *gap* is $I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$ (the
variance-of-the-gap lemma below), and the trace collapses to
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$. The single trap — substituting
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ for the variance of the gap — would produce a
spurious $-\tfrac12 k$; the lemma is stated precisely to forestall it.

*Term A* is the delicate half. The missing-data log-density is now averaged over
completions drawn from the imputation model rather than the truth, and a second-order
expansion in the imputation parameter $\tilde\phi$ yields three pieces. The leading
cross-term gives $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$; the remaining two — an entropy-gradient
term $(A)$ and a cross-entropy-curvature term $(C)$, each $O(1)$ — cancel **only when the
missing and observed units share a conditioning-variable distribution** (MCAR). Under MAR
their sum is a nonzero $O(1)$ design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$, which we isolate as a lemma
inside the Term A argument. (An earlier shortcut wrongly set one piece to zero; a second
wrongly asserted the cancellation in general — both corrected below.)

Summing, $\mathbb{E}[T] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)] - \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)]$, the design-imbalance term vanishing under MCAR.

### Term B $=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (estimation mismatch) {#sec-termB}

This term is elementary and exact to leading order.

**B1.** $\hat\theta_{\text{com}}=\arg\max_\theta\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)$ [D3], so
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\ge\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ and $B\le 0$.

**B2.** A second-order Taylor expansion of $\ell_{\text{com}}$ about its maximizer
$\hat\theta_{\text{com}}$, whose first-order term vanishes since $S_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=0$ [D3]:
$$ B = -\tfrac12(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})^\top \mathcal{J}_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}}) + O_p(n^{-3/2}). $$

**B3.** Take $\mathbb{E}$; replace $\mathcal{J}_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\to_p I_{\text{com}}$ [R1, R2];
apply the quadratic-form identity $a^\top Ba=\operatorname{tr}(Baa^\top)$; both estimators are
asymptotically unbiased for $\theta_0$, so $\mathbb{E}[\cdot\,(\cdot)^\top]$ is the variance of the gap:
$$ \mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})\big)+O(n^{-3/2}). $$

**B4.** Substitute L2, then reduce the trace:
$$ \mathbb{E}[B] = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{com}}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1})\big)\ \text{[L2]}\ = -\tfrac12\big(\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}})-k\big)\ \text{(trace algebra)}\ = -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})\ \text{[D8]}. $$ {#eq-termB}

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-termb (`B + tr(RIV)/2 == 0`).
:::

**Which information.** Term B is governed by the variance of the estimator *gap* (L2),
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$,
where $I_{\text{obs}}$ is the observed-data information (@sec-riv). Graded against the **naive**
block-diagonal information the gap variance looked $\approx 10\%$ inflated
($\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{com}}\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\text{gap}))\approx 6.0$ vs $5.5$ at
$n=2000$, `phase6-info/`); graded against the observed-data information it is the single
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, with no residual gap (note after @eq-combine).

### Term A $=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (imputation bias) {#sec-termA}

**A1.** Factor the complete-data log-likelihood
$\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta;Y)=\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y_{\text{obs}})+\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta)$,
$\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta):=\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$.
The $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ parts cancel in $A$ (D9, D10), leaving

::: {.column-margin}
**Source.** @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997, eq (3.2): complete-data factorization [<a href="#sec-src-schafer"><code>:944</code></a>].
:::
$$ A = \mathbb{E}\big[\,\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\tilde\phi}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})] - \ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\,\big]. $$

**A2.** By iterated expectation over the true completion $Y_{\text{mis}}\sim\theta_0$,
$$ A = \mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{obs}}}\big[\,\mathbb{E}_{\tilde\phi}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})] - \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})]\,\big], $$
the discrepancy between averaging the missing-data log-density under the *fitted* $\tilde\phi$
versus the truth $\theta_0$. These two inner expectations would cancel were $\tilde\phi=\theta_0$
(the oracle case, whence $\mathbb{E}[A]=0$ by the tower property — @sec-qfun); $A$ is nonzero
precisely because the imputations use $\tilde\phi\approx\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}\neq\theta_0$.

**A3.** Second-order Taylor in $\tilde\phi$ about $\theta_0$, using the score-function
identity $\partial_\phi\mathbb{E}_\phi[g]\big|_{\theta_0}=\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[g\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}]$
[R1, differentiate under the integral] and Bartlett
$\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}^\top]=I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$
[R1, D7], then expanding $\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})$ about $\theta_0$, gives three pieces:
$$ A = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top\alpha]}_{(A)} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0)]}_{(B)\ \text{main}} + \underbrace{\tfrac12\mathbb{E}[(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)^\top H_{\phi\phi}(\tilde\phi-\theta_0)]}_{(C)}. $$

::: {.callout-warning}
## $\alpha$ is not zero (do not invoke Gibbs)
$\alpha = \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)] = \operatorname{Cov}_{\theta_0}(\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}},S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}) = -\mathcal{E}'(\theta_0)$,
the gradient of the conditional entropy — generally nonzero (e.g. $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$:
$\alpha_\mu=0$, $\alpha_{\sigma^2}=-n_{\text{mis}}/2\sigma^2\neq 0$). Gibbs' inequality
concerns varying the *evaluation* point of $\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\log p_\theta]$; $\alpha$
varies the *sampling* distribution. The two are different functions — an earlier
shortcut that set $\alpha=0$ was wrong (v4.1 correction).
:::

**A4 (the $(A)+(C)$ term).** Pieces $(A)$ and $(C)$ are each $O(1)$ — $(A)$ pairs the bias of
$\tilde\phi$ with the entropy gradient, $(C)$ the curvature of the conditional cross-entropy with
$\operatorname{Var}(\tilde\phi)$, linked through the information geometry of
$P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$. Their **leading parts cancel iff the missing and
observed units share a conditioning-variable distribution**:
$$ (A)+(C) = \begin{cases} O(n^{-1}) & \text{MCAR } (Q_{\text{mis}}=Q_{\text{obs}}),\\[2pt] \tfrac{n_{\text{mis}}}{n_{\text{obs}}}\big[1-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(Q_{\text{mis}}Q_{\text{obs}}^{-1})\big]+O(n^{-1}) & \text{MAR (bivariate monotone).} \end{cases} $$
The MAR case is a nonzero $O(1)$ **design-imbalance** correction; the mechanism, the closed-form
MCAR cases, and the MAR formula are in the lemma below. So the main piece $(B)$ gives the leading
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, plus this design-imbalance term:
$A = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})\big)+[(A)+(C)]+O(n^{-1})$.

::: {.column-margin}
`[derived]` Mechanism + two closed-form families in the lemma below; closed forms
computed in the companion MI-IC derivation (v4, Steps 6-7) and reproduced here so this
document is self-contained; the bivariate-monotone $(A)+(C)$ closed form is independently
re-derived by a second computer-algebra system (Wolfram) in
`verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac.py`. $A$ and $B$ both calibrate to the single observed-data RIV (note after
@eq-combine), now augmented by the $(A)+(C)$ design-imbalance term under MAR (@sec-termA lemma).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Lemma (the $(A)+(C)$ term: MCAR cancellation, MAR design-imbalance)

Let $f_0(\phi):=\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\sim\phi}\big[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\big]$ be the
conditional **cross-entropy** — the expected missing-data log-density evaluated at the
truth $\theta_0$, over completions drawn from $\phi$. With $\delta:=\tilde\phi-\theta_0$,
pieces $(A)$ and $(C)$ are exactly the first- and second-order terms of the Taylor
expansion of $f_0$ about $\theta_0$:
$$ (A)+(C) = \mathbb{E}[\delta^\top\nabla f_0(\theta_0)] + \tfrac12\mathbb{E}[\delta^\top\nabla^2 f_0(\theta_0)\delta] + O(n^{-3/2}), $$
with $\nabla f_0(\theta_0)=\alpha$ (the entropy gradient of A3) and
$\nabla^2 f_0(\theta_0)=H_{\phi\phi}$.

**Curvature identity** [R1]. Differentiating
$\nabla f_0(\phi)=\mathbb{E}_\phi[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\phi)]$
once more under the integral (R1) and using $\nabla_\phi S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=-\mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$:
$$ H_{\phi\phi} = \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}\!\big[\ell_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}(\theta_0)\,(S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}S_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}^\top - \mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})\big],\qquad \mathcal{J}_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}=-\nabla^2_\phi\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\phi). $$

**Order counting.** Under proper MI centered at $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ [R5, L1],
$\mathbb{E}[\delta]=\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0]=O(n^{-1})$ (MLE bias) and
$\operatorname{Var}(\delta)=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}=O(n^{-1})$, while $\alpha=O(n)$ and
$H_{\phi\phi}=O(n)$ (both extensive in $n_{\text{mis}}$). Hence
$$ (A)=\mathbb{E}[\delta]^\top\alpha = O(1),\qquad (C)=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(H_{\phi\phi}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big)+O(n^{-1}) = O(1) $$
are two $O(1)$ pieces of opposite sign. $(A)$ pairs the entropy gradient
$\alpha=-\mathcal{E}'(\theta_0)$ with the MLE bias of $\tilde\phi$ (scaling with
$1/n_{\text{obs}}$); $(C)$ pairs the cross-entropy curvature $H_{\phi\phi}$ with
$\operatorname{Var}(\tilde\phi)$ (scaling with the inverse design moment $Q_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$),
over $P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\theta)$. Writing $Q_{\text{obs}},Q_{\text{mis}}$ for the
conditioning-variable second-moment matrices over the observed vs missing units, their leading
parts cancel **iff the per-unit design moments match**,
$Q_{\text{mis}}/n_{\text{mis}}=Q_{\text{obs}}/n_{\text{obs}}$ — i.e. under MCAR. When they differ
(MAR) the residual is the nonzero $O(1)$ design-imbalance term
$$ (A)+(C) = \tfrac{n_{\text{mis}}}{n_{\text{obs}}}-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(M_{\text{mis}}M_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big) = \tfrac{n_{\text{mis}}}{n_{\text{obs}}}\big[1-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(Q_{\text{mis}}Q_{\text{obs}}^{-1})\big]+O(n^{-1}), $$
with $M_\bullet=\sum_{\text{units}}[1,X_2][1,X_2]^\top=n_\bullet Q_\bullet$, derived in closed form
for the bivariate monotone MVN.

**Closed-form checks.** With no conditioning structure the family is MCAR-equivalent and
$(A)+(C)$ cancels to leading order (MLE imputation $\tilde\phi=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$;
$n_{\text{obs}}$ observed, $n_{\text{mis}}$ missing):

| Family (no conditioning ⇒ MCAR) | $(A)$ | $(C)=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(H_{\phi\phi}I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})$ | $(A)+(C)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | $+\,n_{\text{mis}}/(2n_{\text{obs}})$ | $-\,n_{\text{mis}}/(2n_{\text{obs}})$ | $0$ (exact) |
| $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ | $+\,n_{\text{mis}}/(n_{\text{obs}}-1)$ | $-\,n_{\text{mis}}/n_{\text{obs}}$ | $n_{\text{mis}}/[n_{\text{obs}}(n_{\text{obs}}-1)]=O(n^{-1})$ |

A univariate $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ *cannot* be MAR — there is no second variable to select on — so
these are necessarily the cancelling cases. Under MAR **with** conditioning the cancellation fails
by the design-imbalance amount above: the bivariate-monotone formula matches the simulation across
selection strength $b$ (`verification/term-a-mar-diagnostic.R`, study S1), and the 4-variate ampute
MAR confirms a material $O(1)$ residual (clean phase-8 sweep, $R{=}40\text{k}$,
`_modules/term-a-mar-correction.R`: non-monotone $-0.34\,(n{=}800)\to-0.46\,(n{=}1500)$, monotone
$-0.18\to-0.27$, both increasing with $n$). A leading-order analytic approximation gives $\approx-0.22$
(right sign and order); the **reported** value uses the realized (observed) information of
@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 — the principled MAR convention — and is the empirical
$-0.34\to-0.46$. The realized RIV exceeds the naive one by the $O(1)$ **realized-information
correction** $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ (the observed-minus-naive information gap), derived in closed form
below.

**General-MVN closed form (monotone).** The exact Term-A integrand is, per unit $i$ with missing
block $M_i$ and conditional moments $m_{\theta,i},S_{\theta,i}$ under $\theta$,
$$ A = \sum_{i:\,M_i\neq\varnothing}\tfrac12\Big[(m_{0,i}-m_{\hat\theta,i})^\top S_{\hat\theta,i}^{-1}(m_{0,i}-m_{\hat\theta,i}) + \operatorname{tr}(S_{\hat\theta,i}^{-1}S_{0,i}) - |M_i|\Big], $$
verified line-by-line against `barQ_fiml_analytic` (it is exactly the Rao-Blackwellized
$A_{\text{rb}}$). Let $f_0(\phi)=\sum_{i:\text{mis}}\mathbb{E}_{X_{M_i}\sim\phi}[\log p(X;\theta_0)]$
be the conditional **cross-entropy** — the imputation slot of $A$ with the evaluation point held at
$\theta_0$. Expanding $f_0$ to second order in $\delta=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0$ gives the
clean two-piece form
$$ (A)+(C)=\underbrace{\alpha^\top\mathbb{E}[\delta]}_{(A)}+\underbrace{\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathcal H_\phi\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big)}_{(C)},\qquad \alpha=\nabla_\phi f_0|_{\theta_0},\ \ \mathcal H_\phi=\nabla^2_\phi f_0|_{\theta_0}, $$
where $\mathcal H_\phi$ is the **cross-entropy curvature** (the imputation-slot Hessian); the
*evaluation*-slot curvature of $A$ supplies the leading $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$, so to this
order no $-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ correction enters. That eval–impute cross-curvature is, by
Louis, the conditional missing information $\mathcal C=\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}U_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}^\top]$,
which under MAR is the **selected** missing information (the incomplete units are a selected subsample).
Because the paper's RIV uses the **realized** observed information — for which
$I_{\text{com}}-I_{\text{obs}}^{O}$ is exactly that selected missing information — the cancellation
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal C\,I_{\text{obs}}^{O,-1})=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is **exact under
the realized (observed-information) convention**. The naive expected-information RIV is the *smaller*
one (Appendix C), and differs from it by the $O(1)$ correction $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ below; that
convention difference separates the measured naive value $+0.1\to+0.3$ from the realized $-0.34\to-0.46$. The entropy gradient is
**covariance-only** ($\partial S_i/\partial\mu=0\Rightarrow\alpha_\mu=0$), with closed form
$$ \alpha_a = -\tfrac12\!\!\sum_{i:\text{mis}}\!\operatorname{tr}\!\big(S_{0,i}^{-1}\,\partial_a S_i\big),\quad \partial_a S_i=\Delta_{MM}-\Delta_{MO}B^\top-B\Delta_{OM}+B\Delta_{OO}B^\top,\ \ B=\Sigma_{MO}\Sigma_{OO}^{-1}, $$
($\partial_a$ the symmetric perturbation of $\Sigma$-coordinate $a$, $\Delta=\partial_a\Sigma$). It
matches the finite-difference gradient to $3\times10^{-5}$, and $\nabla_\phi f_0=\alpha$ confirms
$\mathcal H_\phi$ is the correct curvature. At $\theta_0$ the block-inverse identity
$(\Sigma_0^{-1}\bar e)_M=0$ (with $\bar e=(e_O;\,m_\phi-\mu_{0,M})$, $e_O=y_O-\mu_{0,O}$) collapses
the mean part of $\mathcal H_\phi$ to a Gauss–Newton form, giving the explicit per-unit Hessian
$$ \mathcal H_\phi=\sum_{i:\text{mis}}\Big[-(\partial_\phi m_i)^\top S_{0,i}^{-1}(\partial_\phi m_i)+\tfrac12\,\partial^2_\Sigma\operatorname{tr}\!\big(S_{0,i}^{-1}S_{\cdot,i}\big)\Big], $$
with the conditional-mean Jacobian $\partial_\phi m_i$ ($\mu$-block $I_M,-B$ deterministic; $\Sigma$-block
$G_a\Sigma_{OO}^{-1}e_O$, $G_a=\Delta_{MO}-B\Delta_{OO}$) and $\partial^2_\Sigma\operatorname{tr}$ the
Schur second differential. Taking expectations, every block reduces to the per-pattern **selected
moments** of $e_O$ ($\mathbb E[e_O\mid P]$, $\mathbb E[e_Oe_O^\top\mid P]$), so $\mathcal H_\phi$ — like
$\alpha$ and $b_\Sigma$ — is analytic (no finite differences). It is validated against a
finite-difference Hessian (deterministic $\mu$-block to $0.2\%$; $(C)$ to $3\times10^{-3}$).

The one nonstandard ingredient is $\mathbb{E}[\delta]$, the **second-order FIML MLE bias of
$\hat\Sigma$ under MAR**; only its $\Sigma$-block enters, since $\alpha$ is covariance-only. For a
**monotone** pattern the observed-data likelihood factors into a sequence of complete-data regressions
of block $j$ on blocks $1{:}j{-}1$, each fit on the $r_j=f_j n$ units observed through step $j$
[@andersonIntroductionMultivariateStatistical2003; @littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019;
@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997]. Because each step's regression slope is conditionally
unbiased given its predictors ($\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\beta_j,\hat\Sigma_{<j})=0$), the per-step
biases combine **without cross-coupling** into a closed recursion for
$b_\Sigma:=\lim_n n\,\mathbb{E}[\hat\Sigma_{\text{obs}}-\Sigma_0]$:
$$ b_{11}=-\sigma_{11},\quad b_{j,P}=\beta_j\,b_{P,P},\quad b_{jj}=-\frac{j}{f_j}\sigma_{jj\cdot<j}+\beta_j\,b_{P,P}\,\beta_j^\top+\frac{\sigma_{jj\cdot<j}}{f_j}\operatorname{tr}\!\big(\Sigma_{PP}\,(\Sigma^{(j)}_{PP})^{-1}\big), $$
$P=1{:}j{-}1$, $\beta_j=\Sigma_{j,P}\Sigma_{PP}^{-1}$, $\sigma_{jj\cdot<j}$ the step-$j$ residual
variance, and $\Sigma^{(j)}_{PP}=\operatorname{Cov}(X_P\mid\text{observed through }j)$ the **selected**
(design-imbalanced) predictor covariance. The first two terms are the classical regression-residual
MLE bias and its recursive propagation through the sweep; the third is the design imbalance, which
collapses to $(j{-}1)\sigma_{jj\cdot<j}/f_j$ — and the whole correction to **zero** — under MCAR,
where $\Sigma^{(j)}_{PP}=\Sigma_{PP}$. (The exact small-sample bias of $\hat\Sigma$ under monotone
missingness is classical [@hyodoestimation; @kandaBasicPropertiesMles1998]; this recursion is its
$n\to\infty$ coefficient for the FIML estimator.)

Assembling the three ingredients, the leading-order analytic approximation to $(A)+(C)$ is the
$n$-independent constant $\alpha^\top b_\Sigma+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal H_\phi I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})$.
For the four-variate monotone-MAR design (@sec-alg) it evaluates to
$$ (A)+(C)_{\text{approx}}=\underbrace{+0.54}_{\alpha^\top b_\Sigma}\ \underbrace{-0.76}_{\frac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal H_\phi I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})}\ \approx\ -0.22, $$
with the **MCAR control returning $\approx0$** (`verification/term-a-mar-closedform.R`) — the correct
sign and $O(1)$ order of the realized value, undershooting its magnitude (a second-order assembly that
carries population moments misses higher-order realized-information curvature).

**Value: settled to the observed-information convention.** Which information enters the RIV is not a
free choice. Under MAR the principled object is the **observed** (realized) information: the naive
expected information requires correctly specifying the missingness mechanism and is otherwise biased,
the classical Kenward–Molenberghs recommendation — "provided the observed information matrix is used,
conventional likelihood based frequentist inference is applicable in the MAR setting"
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §5], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:544</code></a>. The
paper's RIV (@sec-riv, Appendix C) and the empirically measured $(A)+(C)$ both use it, so the
**reported value is the realized one**, $-0.34\to-0.46$ (non-monotone) and $-0.18\to-0.27$ (monotone)
over $n=800,1500$.

::: {.column-margin}
`[derived]` Realized-information correction $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ in closed form: the analytic
derivation from the per-pattern selected moments (`verification/term-ac-realized-info.R`; the analytic
$\mathbb{E}[I_{\text{obs}}^{O}]$ reproduces the realized Hessian to $10^{-11}$). A **blind, off-lineage**
cross-check by a second model family (GPT-5.5) independently reproduces the $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$
**block structure** and the $O(1)$-under-MAR / $0$-under-MCAR **order** — the latter correcting a prior
cross-model blind spot (an erroneous $O(n^{-1})$ call). The trace functional and the choice of reference
were supplied in the prompt, not produced blind (`todo/019`). Transparent provenance per the authorship
model.
:::

How much the convention matters is itself an $O(1)$ quantity — the **realized-information correction**
$$ \Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}=\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{obs}}^{N,-1}I_{\text{com}}\big)-\operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbb{E}[I_{\text{obs}}^{O}]^{-1}I_{\text{com}}\big)=\operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{obs}}^{N,-1}\,\big(\mathbb{E}[I_{\text{obs}}^{O}]-I_{\text{obs}}^{N}\big)\,\mathbb{E}[I_{\text{obs}}^{O}]^{-1}I_{\text{com}}\big), $$
the RIV-trace gap between the naive information $I_{\text{obs}}^{N}$ (block-diagonal, population
$\Sigma_{OO}$) and the realized $I_{\text{obs}}^{O}$. By Appendix C, $\mathbb{E}[I_{\text{obs}}^{O}]-I_{\text{obs}}^{N}$
has a zero $\mu\mu$ block, a $\mu\times\operatorname{vech}\Sigma$ **cross block** carried by the
per-pattern selection shift $\mathbb{E}[e_O\mid P]$ (the K&M term that the block-diagonal $I_{\text{obs}}^{N}$
sets to zero), and a $\operatorname{vech}\Sigma$ block carried by the selected-minus-population second
moment $\mathbb{E}[e_Oe_O^\top\mid P]-\Sigma_{OO}$ — both nonzero under MAR and **exactly zero under
MCAR**. So $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ is $O(1)$ under MAR and $0$ under MCAR. Evaluated in closed form from
the selected moments (no fitting), it is $n$-stable at $\approx-0.55$ (non-monotone) and $\approx-0.44$
(monotone), with the MCAR control $\to0$ (`verification/term-ac-realized-info.R`); a paired lavaan-FIML
measurement at the fit returns the same gap, approaching $\approx-0.55$ as $n$ grows
($-0.627,-0.582,-0.569$ at $n=800,1500,3000$; the residual at $-0.569$ being the
$\hat\theta$-vs-$\theta_0$ offset; `verification/scratch-rem-realized-expected-lavaan.R`).

Switching from the (biased) naive to the (principled) observed information therefore shifts $(A)+(C)$
by exactly $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$,
$$ (A)+(C)_{\text{realized}}=(A)+(C)_{\text{naive}}+\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}, $$
where $(A)+(C)_{\text{naive}}=\mathbb{E}[A_{\text{rb}}]-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}^{N})\approx+0.1\to+0.3$
is the expected-information-convention value — the anchored, higher-variance leg (the low-variance
carriers are the paired gap and the analytic $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$). The realized value is the
empirical $-0.34\to-0.46$; naive and realized are tied by $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ as a near-identity (they
share $A_{\text{rb}}$, so $\mathrm{rem}_{\text{real}}\equiv\mathrm{rem}_{\text{exp}}+\mathrm{gap}$), so the
genuine independent check is the analytic $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}\approx-0.55$ against the measured paired
gap $\approx-0.58$. What is firm:
$(A)+(C)$ is a nonzero $O(1)$ under MAR, vanishes under MCAR, and equals the realized value
$-0.34\to-0.46$ under the principled observed-information convention.

**Scope.** The closed-form recursion above is for **monotone** MVN missingness, where the
observed-data likelihood factors into complete-data regressions. For **non-monotone** patterns it
does not factor, so $b_\Sigma$ has no such recursion; it instead follows from the general second-order
(Cox–Snell) bias of the maximum-likelihood estimate
[@coxGeneralDefinitionResiduals1968; @cordeiroBiasCorrectionARMA1994], evaluated from the first three
derivatives of the (non-factoring) observed-data log-likelihood — the multivariate-normal recipe of
@patriotaBiasCorrectionMultivariate2009, adapted to the pattern-mixed observed-data score. For the
minimal bivariate non-monotone design — complete / $X_1$-missing / $X_2$-missing units under an
additive half-sigmoid MAR mechanism — this $b_\Sigma$ is now **derived** and independently confirmed
by two computer-algebra systems that agree to $\sim10^{-9}$ (Wolfram adaptive quadrature and SymPy
Gauss–Hermite, `verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone{,_sympy}.py`). It satisfies the
score ($\mathbb{E}[U]=0$) and information ($\mathbb{E}[UU^\top]=\mathcal I$) identities to machine
precision, reduces **exactly** to the monotone closed form when the $X_2$-missing group is removed
($b_{\sigma_{22}}=-\sigma_{22}$, $b_{\sigma_{12}}=-\sigma_{12}$, and the regression-residual recursion
for $b_{\sigma_{11}}$), and matches a direct Monte-Carlo measurement of $n\,\mathbb{E}[\hat\Sigma-\Sigma]$
in every component within Monte-Carlo error (e.g. $b_{\sigma_{11}}=-1.19$ analytic vs $-1.21\pm0.16$
at $n{=}800$). Assembling $(A)+(C)=\alpha^\top b_\Sigma+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal H_\phi
I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})$ with this $b_\Sigma$ then gives a nonzero $O(1)$ design-imbalance that grows from
$\approx0$ under MCAR to $\approx-0.10$ as selection strengthens, with the direct
$A_{\text{rb}}-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ remainder corroborating its sign and order
($\approx-0.07$, the leading-order-vs-realized-information gap matching the monotone case) and the
MCAR control returning $\approx0$ (`verification/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone.R`). The same Cox–Snell
$b_\Sigma$ is now carried to **general $p$**, including the four-variate phase-8 ampute design
($k{=}14$; `verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone_genp.py`), where it reduces exactly to
the bivariate value and passes the score/information identities. The four-variate assembly's
leading-order approximation gives $\approx-0.22$ (`verification/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone_4v.R`; right
sign and order); the **reported** value uses the realized (observed) information of
@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 and is the empirically measured remainder $-0.34\to-0.46$. The
realized RIV exceeds the naive one by the $O(1)$ realized-information correction
$\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}\approx-0.55$ (non-monotone, the observed-minus-naive information gap), the
selected-vs-population information effect under MAR, derived in closed form (@sec-termA; its block
structure and $O(1)$ order independently cross-checked off-lineage); it is **not** a $b_\Sigma$ shortfall
($b_\Sigma$ is derived and nearly selection-invariant). An earlier version asserted $(A)+(C)=O(n^{-1})$ "for any regular conditional
family"; that holds **only under MCAR** and is **retracted** for MAR. Independent
leading-order support that the *main* piece gives $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$: the FIML $A$
matches the R code to machine precision (@lst-mc-fiml, @lst-mc-mcse), and the high-$R$ phase-6
diagnostic graded against the observed-data RIV (@sec-riv) shows $A$ and $B$ calibrate to the single
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ once the $(A)+(C)$ residual is accounted for (note after
@eq-combine).
:::

**A5.** The cross-term needs the imputer's draws centered at $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ with the
observed-data covariance. Under congeniality [R4] the imputer's posterior is the Bayesian posterior
for $\theta\mid Y_{\text{obs}}$, which by Bernstein–von Mises under regularity [R1;
@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a, §10.2] is asymptotically $N(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}},I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})$.
This supplies the **posterior alignment** $\mathbb{E}[\tilde\phi\mid Y_{\text{obs}}]=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}+o_P(n^{-1/2})$
and, writing $\tilde\phi=\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}+\Delta\tilde\phi$, the posterior-draw orthogonality
$\operatorname{Cov}(\Delta\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=0$, so
$\operatorname{Cov}(\tilde\phi,\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$
