[L1]. Properness [R5] alone does not fix this centering — it is the congeniality/BvM alignment that
does; likewise the matching $T_\infty=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (L4) rests on self-efficiency [R6], not R5.
Hence, carrying the design-imbalance term of A4,
$$ \mathbb{E}[A] = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\,I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}\big) + [(A)+(C)] + O(n^{-1}) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)] + O(n^{-1})\ \text{[D7, D8]}, $$ {#eq-termA}
with $(A)+(C)=0$ under MCAR (so $\mathbb{E}[A]=\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$) and the nonzero
$O(1)$ design-imbalance term of A4 under MAR. (The MCAR cancellation here is between two nonzero
pieces and so uses $\alpha\neq0$ — an estimated scale; for a known-scale, location-only fit piece
$(C)$ instead survives at $-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ even under MCAR, halving
$\mathbb{E}[A]$. See the scope remark after the theorem.)

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-terma (`A - tr(RIV) == 0`) for the **leading-order** trace
identity; the $(A)+(C)$ design-imbalance term is separate. The **non-monotone** $b_\Sigma$ is derived
via Cox–Snell, confirmed by two computer-algebra systems plus direct Monte-Carlo
(`verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone{,_sympy}.py`,
`verification/verify_term_ac_nonmonotone.R`) and extended to general $p$ (`..._genp.py`, `..._4v.R`).
The $(A)+(C)$ **value** is settled to the observed-information convention
[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998]: a leading-order approximation gives $\approx-0.22$ (right
sign and order), and the **reported** realized value is the empirically measured remainder,
$-0.18\to-0.27$ (monotone) and $-0.34\to-0.46$ (non-monotone). The realized RIV exceeds the naive one
by the $O(1)$ realized-information correction $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ ($\approx-0.55$ non-monotone),
derived two ways (@sec-termA). Firm: $(A)+(C)$ is $O(1)$ under MAR, $\approx0$ under MCAR. The MVN FIML instantiation of $\bar Q_\infty$ matches the R
code to machine precision (@lst-mc-fiml).
:::

**Which information.** Term A uses
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (L1), with $I_{\text{obs}}$ the
observed-data information; the high-$R$ diagnostic confirms $\mathbb{E}[A]$ tracks
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ on this same observed-data information **up to the $(A)+(C)$
design-imbalance term** (zero under MCAR, $O(1)$ under MAR) — the single RIV being the one Term B
also calibrates to (@sec-riv; note after @eq-combine).

### Combine: $T = A + B = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)]$ {#sec-combine}

$$ \mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}[A]+\mathbb{E}[B] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)] - \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + [(A)+(C)] + O(n^{-1}) $$ {#eq-combine}
[D10-D12], combining the Term A (@eq-termA) and Term B (@eq-termB) results. Under MCAR
$(A)+(C)=0$ and $\mathbb{E}[T]=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$; under MAR the
design-imbalance term remains — a nonzero $O(1)$ whose structure is derived (entropy gradient,
cross-entropy curvature, and the now general-$p$ Cox–Snell $b_\Sigma$) and whose numerical value is
settled to the observed-information convention [@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998]: the realized
$-0.34\to-0.46$, with the realized-vs-naive information gap $\Delta_{\mathrm{KM}}$ derived in closed
form (@sec-termA), its block structure and $O(1)$ order independently cross-checked by a blind
off-lineage model. $\qquad\blacksquare$

::: {.column-margin}
`[mc]` @lst-mc-combine (`T - tr(RIV)/2 == 0`), the **leading-order** identity (MCAR, or before
adding the $(A)+(C)$ term).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Remark: the net bias is the conditional-entropy plug-in bias

The same total has a second, more compact reading that ties the pieces together. Let
$C_n(\theta)=\sum_i\mathbb{E}_\theta[\log P(Y_{\text{mis},i}\mid Y_{\text{obs},i},\theta)\mid Y_{\text{obs},i}]$
be the (negative) conditional entropy of the missing block given the observed block; for the
multivariate normal $C_n(\theta)=-\tfrac12\sum_i[\,|M_i|\log 2\pi e+\log|\Sigma_{M\mid O,i}(\theta)|\,]$,
a function of $\Sigma$ alone. Evaluating this entropy at the estimate rather than the truth incurs the
**plug-in bias** $\Delta_n=\mathbb{E}[C_n(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})-C_n(\theta_0)]$, and to leading order
$$ \mathbb{E}[T]=\Delta_n=+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+[(A)+(C)]+O(n^{-1}), $$
so the conditional-entropy story and the $(A)/(B\text{-main})/(C)$ split of @sec-termA are the *same*
bias, regrouped. The bridge is a single identity for the entropy curvature at the truth,
$$ \nabla^2 C_n(\theta_0)=\underbrace{H_{\phi\phi}}_{\text{cross-entropy curvature}}+\underbrace{I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}}_{\text{conditional information}}, $$
read off by the chain rule from $C_n(\theta)=F(\theta,\theta)$, where
$F(\phi,\psi)=\mathbb{E}_{Y_{\text{mis}}\sim\phi}[\log P(Y_{\text{mis}}\mid Y_{\text{obs}},\psi)]$ has
sampling-slot Hessian $H_{\phi\phi}$, evaluation-slot Hessian $-I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$, and mixed
block $+I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ (Bartlett). Taylor-expanding $\Delta_n$ in
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\theta_0$ then sends its first-order part to $(A)=\alpha^\top\mathbb{E}[\delta]$
(since $\nabla C_n(\theta_0)=\alpha$) and its second-order part to
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(H_{\phi\phi}I_{\text{obs}}^{-1})+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})=(C)+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$.
This unifies the scope cases of @sec-thm: with an estimated covariance
$\Delta_n=\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})+[(A)+(C)]$, whereas for a known scale $C_n$ is
parameter-free, $\Delta_n=0$, and the $\mu$-block of the very same split *is* the Isserlis identity
$H_{\phi\phi}=-I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}$ used there. The reading is also unambiguous: the full
normalized normal density carries no parameter-free, $Y_{\text{mis}}$-dependent additive constant
(every $Y$-quadratic enters through $\Sigma^{-1}$), so $T$ — and with it $\Delta_n$ — is convention-free.
We keep $+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ as the headline; $\mathbb{E}[T]=\Delta_n$ is recorded
as a consistency check, confirmed symbolically by two computer-algebra systems and by direct Monte
Carlo (`verification/cas-wolfram/verify_term_ac{,_sympy}.py`,
`verification/rederivation-L0-fitted-vs-oracle.R`).
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Both terms calibrate to the single observed-data RIV
This callout concerns *which information defines the RIV* — a separate matter from the $(A)+(C)$
term (@sec-termA), which is an additional Term-A correction, not a re-grading of the RIV. Setting
$(A)+(C)$ aside (it is $0$ under MCAR), the leading constants
$\mathbb{E}[A]=+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and $\mathbb{E}[B]=-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
hold to $O(n^{-1})$ against **one** RIV — the observed-data RIV of @sec-riv, with
$I_{\text{obs}}$ the unconditional information (estimated by the observed information). Term A is
governed by $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}$ (L1) and Term B by
$\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}-\hat\theta_{\text{com}})=I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}-I_{\text{com}}^{-1}$
(L2); both reduce to the same $\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})$.

A high-$R$ diagnostic confirms this directly: graded against the observed-data RIV, both
$\mathbb{E}[A]-\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ and $\mathbb{E}[B]+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
are small and consistent with $O(n^{-1})$, and the asymptotic inputs L1-L3 hold against that same
information (`verification/lemma-revalidation.R`; also `run_all.R` phase 6; `todo/008`). The earlier reading —
that the two terms attach to *different* (expected vs sample) RIVs with a slowly closing gap — was
an artifact of grading the bias against the **naive**, block-diagonal information
(`fisher_info_obs_mvn`), which is the MCAR form and omits the MAR cross term (@sec-riv,
Appendix C; [@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998]). Against the correct observed-data
information there is a single RIV and no residual gap.

Three layers of evidence: this proof (the algebra), the inline symbolic verification
(@sec-verify), and the pre-registered + diagnostic Monte Carlo study (`verification/`,
including the phase-6 information diagnostic).

**Robustness to the imputation engine.** The sample RIV in that diagnostic is built from the
imputations actually drawn — here by the EMB sampler, expectation–maximization run on bootstrap
resamples [@honakerAmeliaIIProgram2011], whose imputation posterior is only an asymptotic approximation. The exact alternative is data augmentation, a Markov chain Monte Carlo
scheme whose draws follow the observed-data posterior at any sample size
[@TannerCalculationPosteriorDistributions1987; @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997], but at much higher computational cost. On the most
demanding cells — the non-monotone MAR design with as few as $N=200$ observations, where the bootstrap
approximation is weakest — the sample RIV reproduces the observed-data RIV to within about $2$–$3\%$,
with no widening of the gap as $N$ grows, and the information-criterion model selection agrees between
full-information maximum likelihood and the bootstrap-imputed analysis to within $0.011$. The
proper-imputation results reported here are therefore insensitive to this choice of sampler for the
designs studied (`run_all.R` phases 5–6, `verification/cache/run_all-prod/`).

This robustness is empirical, and we scope it as such. The bootstrap sampler is only *approximately*
Bayesian: it propagates parameter uncertainty by resampling rather than by drawing from the exact
observed-data posterior, and such resampling rests on large-sample arguments whose small-sample
behaviour is, in Little and Rubin's assessment, less assured than the Bayesian alternative
[@littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019, §5.5]. Proper data augmentation
[@TannerCalculationPosteriorDistributions1987; @schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997] removes that approximation at the cost
of a Markov chain.
We observe no resulting discrepancy on the designs examined — including the small-$N$, non-monotone
cells where the approximation is most exposed — but this does not establish engine-independence beyond
them, and a fully Bayesian sampler remains the conservative choice when the fraction of missing
information is large.
:::

---

## Appendix A — Verbatim source passages {#sec-sources}

Consolidated provenance audit, grouped by reference. Each entry reproduces — verbatim
from the `literature/<citekey>.md` Mathpix conversions — the specific passages the
derivation invokes, annotated with the source's own equation/theorem number and the
`literature/<citekey>.md` line. The margin provenance blocks in the preceding sections keep a short
snippet as a re-pagination-stable anchor and link here (`@sec-src-<key>`) for the full
text.

**Fair use and post-review deletion.** These excerpts are reproduced for the transparency
audit that JAIGP norms call for, so a reviewer can check each cited claim against its source
without leaving the document. To keep each quotation interpretable we include a little
surrounding context — the lead-in, the cited statement, and the following clause.
**These verbatim passages are temporary: they will be deleted once the blinded peer-review
process concludes**, leaving only the equation/theorem numbers and the
`literature/<citekey>.md` line references. Should the venue or a reviewer object to verbatim
reproduction at any earlier point, trim each entry to its number and line reference and drop
the quoted text.

### Schafer (1997) — Analysis of Incomplete Multivariate Data {#sec-src-schafer}

[@schaferAnalysisIncompleteMultivariate1997]. Invoked by D1, D4, D6, D7, R1, L3, Term A step A1.

**Complete-data likelihood factorization, eq (3.2)** [`:941`–`:947`].

> Viewing each term in (3.1) as a function of $\theta$, it follows that
> $l(\theta \mid Y)=l(\theta \mid Y_{obs})+\log P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta)+c$, where
> $l(\theta/Y)=\log P(Y \mid \theta)$ denotes the complete-data log likelihood,
> $l(\theta/Y_{obs})$ … the observed-data loglikelihood, and $c$ an arbitrary constant. The
> term $P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta)$, which we shall call the predictive distribution of
> the missing data given $\theta$, plays a central role in EM because it captures the
> interdependence between $Y_{mis}$ and $\theta$.

**EM decomposition of the Q-function** [`:949`–`:964`].

> Because $Y_{mis}$ is unknown we cannot calculate the second term on the right-hand side of
> (3.2), so instead we take the average of (3.2) over the predictive distribution
> $P(Y_{mis} \mid Y_{obs}, \theta^{(t)})$ … This averaging yields
> $Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})=l(\theta \mid Y_{obs})+H(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})+c$, where
> $Q(\theta\mid\theta^{(t)})=\int l(\theta\mid Y)\,P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta^{(t)})\,dY_{mis}$
> and $H(\theta\mid\theta^{(t)})=\int \log P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta)\,P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta^{(t)})\,dY_{mis}$.

**Missing-information principle and the information identity, eq (3.19)** [`:1480`–`:1504`].

> Taking the expectation of this over $P(Y_{mis}\mid Y_{obs},\theta)$, we obtain a
> fundamental relationship: the complete information is equal to the observed information
> plus the missing information. This relationship, called the missing information principle
> by Orchard and Woodbury (1972) … Assuming sufficient regularity to interchange the order
> of differentiation and integration, we can write the complete information as
> $I_c(\theta)=-\partial^2 Q(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$, the observed information as
> $I_o(\theta)=-\partial^2 l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})/\partial\theta^2$, and the missing
> information as $I_m(\theta)=-\partial^2 H(\theta\mid\theta)/\partial\theta^2$, so that
> $I_c(\theta)=I_o(\theta)+I_m(\theta)$ (3.19). Note that each quantity in (3.19) is a
> function of $Y_{obs}$ although this fact has been suppressed in the notation.

**Observed-data MLE** [`:1504`–`:1510`].

> A natural large-sample estimate of the covariance matrix with incomplete data is
> $I_o^{-1}(\hat\theta)$ … where $\hat\theta$ is now the observed-data MLE, the maximizer of
> $l(\theta\mid Y_{obs})$.

**EM rate matrix (fraction of missing information), eq (3.21)** [`:1514`–`:1520`] — cited
for the D8 disambiguation; this is *not* RIV.

> Dempster, Laird and Rubin (1977) established an important connection between these
> information quantities and $D$ … the asymptotic rate matrix of EM … they showed that
> $D=I_c^{-1}(\hat\theta)\, I_m(\hat\theta)$ (3.21) … for a scalar parameter (3.21) implies
> that $D$ is the ratio of the missing information to the complete information. For brevity
> we will call this ratio the fraction of missing information.

### Magnus & Neudecker (2019) — Matrix Differential Calculus {#sec-src-magnus}

[@magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019]. Invoked by D14, D15, and @sec-alg.

**Half-vectorization** [`:1652`] (here for $3\times3$ symmetric $A$).

> $\operatorname{vech}(A)=\left(a_{11}, a_{21}, a_{31}, a_{22}, a_{32}, a_{33}\right)^{\prime}$

**Duplication matrix, eq (23)** [`:1658`], and its inverse [`:1664`].

> $D_{n} \operatorname{vech}(A)=\operatorname{vec} A \quad\left(A=A^{\prime}\right)$

> $\operatorname{vech}(A)=\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1} D_{n}^{\prime} \operatorname{vec} A \quad\left(A=A^{\prime}\right)$

**Inverse of $D_n'(A\otimes A)D_n$** [`:1692`].

> $\left(D_{n}^{\prime}(A \otimes A) D_{n}\right)^{-1}=\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1} D_{n}^{\prime}\left(A^{-1} \otimes A^{-1}\right) D_{n}\left(D_{n}^{\prime} D_{n}\right)^{-1}$

**MVN information matrix for $(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Omega)$** [`:1804`-`:1811`]
($\Omega\!\leftrightarrow\!\Sigma$, $m\!\leftrightarrow\!p$, $n\!\leftrightarrow\!N$ in our D15).

> $\mathcal{F}=n\begin{pmatrix}\Omega^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{2} D_{m}^{\prime}\left(\Omega^{-1} \otimes \Omega^{-1}\right) D_{m}\end{pmatrix}$

### van der Vaart (1998) — Asymptotic Statistics {#sec-src-vaart}

[@vaartAsymptoticStatistics1998a]. Invoked by D5, D6, R1, R2, L1, L3, and @sec-alg.

**Score identities** [`:2320`].

> "$P_{\theta} \dot{\ell}_{\theta}=0$ (scores have mean zero), and $P_{\theta} \ddot{\ell}_{\theta}=-I_{\theta}$ (the curvature of the likelihood is equal to minus the Fisher information)."

**MLE asymptotic normality** [`:2325`].

> $\sqrt{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right) \rightsquigarrow N\left(0, I_{\theta}^{-1}\right)$

**MLE linear (influence-function) expansion, Theorem 5.39** [`:4170`, `:7388`-`:7393`];
the joint full/null-restricted form at `:7392`-`:7393` underpins L3's score covariance.

> $\sqrt{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{\theta}^{-1} \dot{\ell}_{\theta}\left(X_{i}\right)+o_{P_{\theta}}(1)$

**Conditional expectation as $L^2$ projection (Ch 11)** [`:5161`, `:5170`].

> (line 5161) "the conditional expectation is the unique measurable function $E(X \mid Y)$ of $Y$ that satisfies the orthogonality relation $\mathrm{E}(X-\mathrm{E}(X \mid Y)) g(Y)=0$"; (line 5170, Example 11.3) "$\mathrm{EX}=E E(X \mid Y)$ ... 'the expectation of a conditional expectation is the expectation.'"

### Meng (1994) — Multiple-Imputation Inferences with Uncongenial Sources of Input {#sec-src-meng}

[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a]. Invoked by D1, D13, R4, R5, R6, L4.
Complete data $Z_{c}=\{X, Y_{inc}, I\}$, observed data $Z_{o}=\{X, Y_{obs}, I, R_{inc}\}$ [`:95`].

**Definition 1 (congeniality), eqs (2.3.1)-(2.3.2)** [`:102`-`:110`].

> $\left[\widehat{\theta}\left(Z_{o}\right), U\left(Z_{o}\right)\right] \simeq\left[E_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{o}\right], V_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{o}\right]\right]$ &nbsp; (2.3.1)

> $\left[\widehat{\theta}\left(Z_{c}\right), U\left(Z_{c}\right)\right] \simeq\left[E_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{c}\right], V_{f}\left[\theta \mid Z_{c}\right]\right]$ &nbsp; (2.3.2)

**Definition 2 (congeniality to an imputation model), eq (2.3.3)** [`:120`].

> $f\left(Y_{mis} \mid Z_{o}\right)=g\left(Y_{mis} \mid Z_{o}, A\right)$

**Definition 5 (self-efficiency)** [`:450`].

> "A statistical estimation procedure $\widehat{\theta}(\cdot)$ ... is said to be self-efficient ... if there is no $\lambda \in(-\infty, \infty)$ such that the mean-squared error of $\lambda \hat{\theta}\left(W_{o}\right)+(1-\lambda) \hat{\theta}\left(W_{c}\right)$ is less than that of $\widehat{\theta}\left(W_{c}\right)$."

**Lemma 3 and Main result (variance recovery / efficiency)** [`:486`, `:503`-`:511`]; conditions
(a) self-efficiency and (c) "second-moment proper" imputation [`:504`, `:506`].

> "(i) The repeated-imputation estimator is consistent for $\theta$, and is at least as efficient as the analyst's incomplete-data estimator."

### Rubin (1987) — Multiple Imputation for Nonresponse in Surveys {#sec-src-rubin1987}

[@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. Invoked by D8, R5, R7, L4, and the D9 notation-clash callout.

**Pooled estimate and the variance components** [`:2161`, `:2167`, `:2173`, `:2179`].

> $\bar{Q}_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m} \hat{Q}_{* l} / m$ ; &nbsp; $\bar{U}_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m} U_{* l} / m$ ; &nbsp; $B_{m}=\sum_{l=1}^{m}\left(\hat{Q}_{* l}-\bar{Q}_{m}\right)^{t}\left(\hat{Q}_{* l}-\bar{Q}_{m}\right) /(m-1)$ ; &nbsp; $T_{m}=\bar{U}_{m}+\left(1+m^{-1}\right) B_{m}$

**Relative increase in variance due to nonresponse** [`:2194`] (named at `:2191`).

> $r_{m}=\left(1+m^{-1}\right) B_{m} / \bar{U}_{m}$

**Randomization-validity of the infinite-$m$ inference, §4.2 — conditions (4.2.1)-(4.2.2)** [`:3489`]
**and Result 4.1** [`:3563`]. This is the variance-recovery property L4 invokes: under proper MI the
infinite-$m$ variance estimator $T_\infty$ is valid for the repeated-sampling variance $T_0$ of the
complete-data statistic.

> $\left(\bar{Q}_{\infty} \mid X, Y\right) \sim N\left(Q, T_{0}\right)$ &nbsp; (4.2.1); &nbsp; $\left(T_{\infty} \mid X, Y\right) \sim\left(T_{0}, \ll T_{0}\right)$ &nbsp; (4.2.2), "where $Q=Q(X, Y)$ and $T_{0} \equiv T_{0}(X, Y)$ are fixed by the true values of $(X, Y)$ ... When (4.2.1) and (4.2.2) hold, we will say that the repeated-imputation inference is randomization-valid."

> (Result 4.1) "If the Complete-Data Inference Is Randomization-Valid and the Multiple-Imputation Procedure Is Proper, Then the Infinite-$m$ Repeated-Imputation Inference Is Randomization-Valid under the Posited Response Mechanism."

### Rubin (1976) — Inference and Missing Data {#sec-src-rubin1976}

[@rubinInferenceMissingData1976b]. Invoked by D2, R3 (ignorability).

**Ignorability for likelihood / Bayesian inference (abstract)** [`:39`].

> "When making direct-likelihood or Bayesian inferences about $\theta$, it is appropriate to ignore the process that causes missing data if the missing data are missing at random and the parameter of the missing data process is 'distinct' from $\theta$."

### Little & Rubin (2019) — Statistical Analysis with Missing Data, 3rd ed. {#sec-src-little}

[@littleStatisticalAnalysisMissing2019]. Invoked by D1, D2, R3.

**MAR definition** [`:438`-`:444`].

> "missingness depends on $y_{i}$ only through the observed components $y_{(0)i}$ ... The missingness mechanism is then called missing at random (MAR)."

**Ignorability — Definition 6.4 and Theorem 6.1A(a)** [`:3263`, `:3277`].

> (Def 6.4) "The missingness mechanism is called ignorable if inferences for $\theta$ based on the ignorable likelihood ... are the same as inferences ... based on the full likelihood"; (Thm 6.1A(a), parameter distinctness) "the joint parameter space $(\theta, \psi)$ ... is the product of the parameter space $\Omega_{\theta}$ of $\theta$ and the parameter space $\Omega_{\psi}$ of $\psi$."

### Anderson (2003) — An Introduction to Multivariate Statistical Analysis {#sec-src-anderson}

[@andersonIntroductionMultivariateStatistical2003]. Invoked by @sec-alg (Gaussian conditional / Schur complement) and Term A's normal Q-function.

**Conditional covariance (Schur complement)** [`:1582`].

> $\Sigma_{11 \cdot 2}=\Sigma_{11}-\Sigma_{12} \Sigma_{22}^{-1} \Sigma_{21}$

**Conditional mean** [`:1598`]; the $q$-variate normal conditional density $f(x^{(1)} \mid x^{(2)})$ has this mean and covariance $\Sigma_{11\cdot2}$ [`:1595`].

> $\mathscr{E}\left(X^{(1)} \mid x^{(2)}\right)=\mu^{(1)}+\Sigma_{12} \Sigma_{22}^{-1}\left(x^{(2)}-\mu^{(2)}\right)$

### Petersen & Pedersen — The Matrix Cookbook {#sec-src-petersen}

[@petersen2008matrix]. Invoked by @sec-alg (trace and vec identities) and the @eq-trriv trace reduction.

**§1.1 Trace** [`:177`-`:187`]. In our Mathpix conversion this section is a single
unnumbered aligned display, so the Cookbook's own inline equation numbers are **not**
recoverable from the file; these trace identities are therefore cited by section + line.

> $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})=\sum_{i} A_{ii}$ [`:179`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A}^{T})$ [`:181`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B A})$ [`:182`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A})+\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B})$ [`:183`]; &nbsp; $\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{A B C})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{B C A})=\operatorname{Tr}(\mathbf{C A B})$ [`:184`]

**§10.2.2 vec-operator, eqs (520)-(521)** [`:3345`-`:3346`]. Equation numbers confirmed
in-file: the vec rule is cited as "Eq 520" at `:3692`, and "Eq. 524" closes the same
numbered block at `:3353`.

> $\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{A X B})=\left(\mathbf{B}^{T} \otimes \mathbf{A}\right) \operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{X})$ &nbsp; (520)

> $\operatorname{Tr}\left(\mathbf{A}^{T} \mathbf{B}\right)=\operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{A})^{T} \operatorname{vec}(\mathbf{B})$ &nbsp; (521)

### Efron & Hinkley (1978) — Observed versus Expected Fisher Information {#sec-src-efron}

[@efronAssessingAccuracyMaximum1978]. Secondary support for preferring the observed information:
the *observed* information, not its expectation, tracks the finite-sample variance of the MLE. The
MAR-specific reason — that the *unconditional* expected information itself departs from the naive
form — is @kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998 (@sec-src-kenward).

**Observed vs expected Fisher information** [`:99`].

> "The quantity $I(x)$ is aptly called the observed Fisher information by some writers, as distinguished from $\mathscr{I}_{\theta}$, the expected Fisher information. This last name is useful even though $E\left(\mathscr{J}_{\theta}\right) \neq \mathscr{I}_{\theta}$ in general."

**The MLE's variance is the reciprocal of the *observed* information** [`:107`–`:113`].

> "Approximation (1•2), one over the expected Fisher information, would presumably never be applied in practice ... the conditional variance $(1\cdot4)$ is a corresponding property of the observed likelihood: $(1\cdot4)$ is equal to the reciprocal of the observed Fisher information $I(x)$."

**Observed information = negative Hessian** [`:224`].

> "the observed Fisher information $I(x)=-\ddot{l}_{\theta}$"

### Kenward & Molenberghs (1998) — Likelihood Inference When Data Are MAR {#sec-src-kenward}

[@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998]. Invoked by @sec-riv, D6, and Appendix C: under MAR
the unconditional expected information differs from the naive (MCAR) form, the mean–covariance
orthogonality is lost, and the observed information should be used.

**Under MAR the response pattern is not ancillary** [`:89`].

> "under the MAR mechanism $\mathbf{r}$ is not an ancillary statistic for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ ... we are not justified in restricting the sample space from that associated with the pair ( $\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{R}$ )."

**The naive (MCAR) expected information is biased** [`:60`].

> "The expected information in these papers is wrong because the expectation is taken under MCAR, in which the missing value mechanism is independent of the distribution of the outcome data."

**MVN: the mean–covariance cross term, vanishing iff MCAR** [`:374`].

> "In contrast to the naive information these cross-terms do not all vanish, and the orthogonality of mean and variance-covariance parameters is lost under the MAR mechanism. ... the necessary and sufficient condition for the terms in (9) and (10) to be equal to zero is that $\alpha_{0}=\alpha_{1}=0$, the condition defining, as expected, an MCAR mechanism."

**Recommendation: use the observed information** [`:38`, `:544`].

> (abstract) "it is shown that the classical expected information matrix is biased and the use of the observed information matrix is recommended." (§5) "provided the observed information matrix is used, conventional likelihood based frequentist inference is applicable in the MAR setting."

### Pawitan (2001) — In All Likelihood (Ch. 13, robustness) {#sec-src-pawitan}

[@pawitanAllLikelihoodStatistical2013]. Invoked by @sec-riv and the @sec-derivation
notation note: the score-variance $\mathcal{J}$ vs expected-Hessian $\mathcal{I}$ distinction,
information equality under correct specification, and the TIC penalty
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$.

**$\mathcal{J}$ (score variance), $\mathcal{I}$ (expected Hessian); equal under the true model** [`:14618`, `:14624`, `:14627`].

> $\mathcal{J} \equiv E\left(\partial\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta\right)\left(\partial\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta'\right)\big|_{\theta_0}$ [`:14618`]; &nbsp; $\mathcal{I} \equiv -E\,\partial^{2}\log f_{\theta}/\partial\theta\partial\theta'\big|_{\theta_0}$ [`:14624`]; &nbsp; "These two matrices are the same if $f_{\theta_{0}}(x)$ is the true model." [`:14627`]

**Sandwich variance (Theorem 13.2) and information equality** [`:14632`, `:14699`].

> $\sqrt{n}(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0}) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \mathcal{I}^{-1}\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ [`:14632`]; &nbsp; "Assuming the model is correct we get $\mathcal{J}=\mathcal{I}$, and the formula reduces to the usual inverse Fisher information $\mathcal{I}^{-1}$ ... the 'naive' variance formula." [`:14699`]

**Trace corollary $\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1})$ (the TIC penalty)** [`:14707`]; the §13.6 AIC derivation restates $\mathcal{J}_{k}$ at [`:14977`].

> $E\left\{n(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0})'\mathcal{I}(\widehat{\theta}-\theta_{0})\right\} \to \operatorname{trace}\left(\mathcal{I}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\right) = \operatorname{trace}\left(\mathcal{J}\mathcal{I}^{-1}\right)$

### Konishi & Kitagawa (1996) — Generalised Information Criteria {#sec-src-konishi}

[@konishiGeneralisedInformationCriteria1996]. Invoked by @sec-riv: the TIC/GIC
generalization of AIC's penalty *under misspecification* — the misspecification face of the
$\mathcal{J}$-vs-$\mathcal{I}$ distinction, contrasted with our correctly-specified case.

**Theorem 2.1 — bias under possible misspecification** [`:80`–`:90`].

> "Suppose that the parametric family of distributions does not necessarily contain the true distribution ... the asymptotic bias of the log likelihood ... $E_{G}\left\{\eta(X_{n};\hat{G})-\eta(X_{n};G)\right\}=\frac{1}{n} b_{1}(G)+o(1/n)$" [`:80`–`:84`], with $b_{1}(G)=\operatorname{tr}\left\{\int T^{(1)}(z;G)\,\partial\log f(z\mid\theta)/\partial\theta'\big|_{T(G)}\,dG(z)\right\}$ [`:90`].

**GIC = bias-corrected log-likelihood (a trace penalty)** [`:100`–`:101`].

> $\operatorname{GIC}(X_{n};\hat{G}) := -2\sum_{\alpha}\log f(X_{\alpha}\mid\hat{\theta}) + \frac{2}{n}\sum_{\alpha}\operatorname{tr}\left\{T^{(1)}(X_{\alpha};\hat{G})\,\partial\log f(X_{\alpha}\mid\theta)/\partial\theta'\big|_{\hat{\theta}}\right\}$

---

## Appendix B — Numerical and symbolic verification {#sec-verify}

The margin `[mc]` flags throughout the preceding sections link to the executable listings
in this appendix. The code runs inline at render time (SymPy + NumPy); every identity is
checked by reducing `lhs - rhs` to `0` (symbolic) or asserting a match to the R code
`verification/00-setup.R` (numeric, to machine precision). This is the algebraic companion
to the Monte Carlo study (`verification/W1-bias-decomposition.R`), which confirms
$\mathbb{E}[T]\approx+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ at many concrete
configurations; here we check the chain of identities that produces it. The symbolic trace
identities and the MVN covariance-block (Magnus duplication-matrix) inversion are independently
re-verified by a second computer-algebra system in `verification/cas-wolfram/verify_traces.py`
(Wolfram), so these reductions do not rest on a single CAS implementation.

Scope: the trace bookkeeping that combines the asymptotic second-moment inputs into
$+\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is **symbolic** (exact, parameterization-free);
the MVN closed forms for $I_{\text{com}},I_{\text{obs}},\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$
and the FIML Q-function are reimplemented in NumPy and checked **to machine precision**
against the R code on a fixed replicate; and $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is
checked **within Monte-Carlo SE** of the W1 cache $\mathbb{E}[T]$. What Sympy does not
prove are the second-moment inputs themselves (the asymptotic covariances L1-L4); those
are likelihood asymptotics stated as lemmas in @sec-ledger and validated end-to-end by the
Monte Carlo study.

```{python}
import sympy as sp
import numpy as np
from sympy import MatMul, MatAdd, Identity, Trace, Transpose, S
from sympy.matrices.expressions.matexpr import MatrixExpr, MatrixSymbol
from numpy.linalg import inv, slogdet, eigvalsh
from pathlib import Path
import json

print("sympy", sp.__version__, "| numpy", np.__version__)
```

### vech / duplication-matrix machinery {#sec-verify-vech}

MVN running example: $X \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu, \Sigma)$, parameter
$\theta = (\mu,\ \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ of dimension $k = p + p(p+1)/2$ (D1, D14).
Sympy has no vech/duplication operator, so we build $D_p$ explicitly, mirroring
`00-setup.R` (column-major lower triangle).

```{python}
def vech_index_table(p):
    m = np.zeros((p, p), int); c = 0
    for j in range(p):
        for i in range(j, p):
            c += 1; m[i, j] = c; m[j, i] = c
    return m

def duplication_matrix(p, sym=False):
    kk = p * (p + 1) // 2
    D = sp.zeros(p * p, kk) if sym else np.zeros((p * p, kk))
    c = 0
    for j in range(p):
        for i in range(j, p):
            D[j * p + i, c] = 1
            if i != j:
                D[i * p + j, c] = 1
            c += 1
    return D
```

### The trace normal form (load-bearing helper) {#sec-verify-tracenorm}

Sympy's `Trace` does not auto-apply linearity, does not evaluate
`Trace(Identity) = n`, and does not exploit cyclic invariance `Trace(AB) = Trace(BA)`;
worse, `sympy.simplify` on a `Trace` hits a `signsimp`/`cancel` bug and raises. The
helper below (i) expands matrix products inside each trace, (ii) splits `Trace` over
sums, (iii) evaluates `Trace(c*I) = c*n`, and (iv) canonicalizes cyclic factor order so
cyclically-equal traces collapse. Every symbolic identity is checked after `trace_normal`.

```{python}
def symmetric_reduce(expr):
    """Treat every bare MatrixSymbol as symmetric: Transpose(X) -> X."""
    return expr.replace(
        lambda x: isinstance(x, Transpose) and isinstance(x.arg, MatrixSymbol),
        lambda x: x.arg)

def trace_normal(expr, n):
    """Normal form for scalar Trace(MatMul) expressions (avoids sympy.simplify)."""
    expr = sp.expand(symmetric_reduce(sp.expand(expr, trace=True)), trace=True)

    def handle(t):
        m = symmetric_reduce(sp.expand(t.arg))
        if isinstance(m, MatAdd):
            return sum(handle(Trace(a)) for a in m.args)
        coeff = S.One
        if isinstance(m, MatMul):
            for a in m.args:
                if not isinstance(a, MatrixExpr):
                    coeff *= a
            mats = [a for a in m.args
                    if isinstance(a, MatrixExpr) and not isinstance(a, Identity)]
            if not mats:
                return coeff * n
            rotations = [mats[i:] + mats[:i] for i in range(len(mats))]
            best = min(rotations, key=lambda fs: tuple(sp.srepr(x) for x in fs))
            return coeff * Trace(MatMul(*best))
        if isinstance(m, Identity):
            return coeff * n
        return coeff * Trace(m)

    return expr.replace(lambda x: isinstance(x, Trace), handle)
```

### Term-combination identities (abstract, exact) {#sec-verify-terms}

Treat $I_{\text{com}}, I_{\text{obs}}$ as abstract symmetric positive-definite
$n\times n$ matrices ($n=k$). The asymptotic second-moment inputs (Lemmas L1-L4 of
@sec-ledger, proved there, not here) feed the trace bookkeeping. First, `tr(RIV)`
(@eq-trriv) in normal form:

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-trriv
#| lst-cap: "tr(RIV) = tr(I_obs^-1 I_com) - n, in trace normal form (D8)."
n = sp.Symbol('n', positive=True, integer=True)
I_com = sp.MatrixSymbol('I_com', n, n)
I_obs = sp.MatrixSymbol('I_obs', n, n)
I_mis = I_com - I_obs                      # I_{mis|obs} := I_com - I_obs

trRIV = trace_normal(Trace(I_obs.inv() * I_mis), n)
assert sp.expand(trace_normal(Trace(I_obs.inv() * I_com), n) - n - trRIV) == 0
print("tr(RIV)  =", trRIV)                  # = Trace(I_obs^{-1} I_com) - n
```

**Term A** $= +\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — plug L1 into L3, then cyclic invariance
(@eq-termA):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-terma
#| lst-cap: "Term A: A - tr(RIV) reduces to 0 (symbolic)."
A = trace_normal(Trace(I_mis * I_obs.inv()), n)         # (L3) with (L1)
assert sp.expand(A - trRIV) == 0
print("A        =", A, "  -> A - tr(RIV) =", sp.expand(A - trRIV))
```

**Term B** $= -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — plug L2 into L4; the $n$ ($=k$)
terms cancel, leaving **no** dimension constant (@eq-termB; this is the cancellation the
Term B / L2 disambiguation in the body warns about):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-termb
#| lst-cap: "Term B: B + tr(RIV)/2 reduces to 0, no leftover -k/2 (symbolic)."
B = trace_normal(sp.Rational(-1, 2) * Trace(I_com * (I_obs.inv() - I_com.inv())), n)
assert sp.expand(B + trRIV / 2) == 0
print("B        =", B, "  -> B + tr(RIV)/2 =", sp.expand(B + trRIV / 2))
```

**Combine** $T = A + B = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (@eq-combine, @eq-theorem):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-combine
#| lst-cap: "Combine: T = A + B = tr(RIV)/2, exact (symbolic)."
T = trace_normal(A, n) + B
assert sp.expand(T - trRIV / 2) == 0
print("T = A+B  =", sp.expand(T))
print("T - tr(RIV)/2 =", sp.expand(T - trRIV / 2), " <- exact zero")
```

### MVN Fisher-information closed forms (symbolic, $p=2$) {#sec-verify-mvn}

Block structure of $I_{\text{com}}$ (D15): block-diagonal on $(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$
with $I_\mu = N\Sigma^{-1}$ and $I_{\operatorname{vech}\Sigma} = \tfrac{N}{2}D_p^\top(\Sigma^{-1}\otimes\Sigma^{-1})D_p$.

```{python}
s11, s12, s22, Nsym = sp.symbols('sigma11 sigma12 sigma22 N', positive=True)
Sig2 = sp.Matrix([[s11, s12], [s12, s22]])
Sig2_inv = Sig2.inv()
D2 = duplication_matrix(2, sym=True)
I_mu_2 = Nsym * Sig2_inv
I_sig_2 = sp.Rational(1, 2) * Nsym * D2.T * sp.Matrix(
    sp.kronecker_product(Sig2_inv, Sig2_inv)) * D2
I_sig_2 = sp.simplify(I_sig_2)   # safe here: ordinary Matrix, not Trace
I_mu_2, I_sig_2
```

### NumPy reimplementations checked against R {#sec-verify-numpy}

These mirror `fisher_info_com_mvn`, `fisher_info_obs_mvn`, `loglik_mvn`, and
`barQ_fiml_analytic` in `verification/00-setup.R`. Matching the R outputs on a fixed
replicate is the gate that the symbolic construction equals the code the W1 simulations
actually ran.

```{python}
def fisher_com(mu, Sig, N):
    p = len(mu); Si = inv(Sig); D = duplication_matrix(p)
    kk = p + p * (p + 1) // 2; I = np.zeros((kk, kk))
    I[:p, :p] = N * Si
    I[p:, p:] = (N / 2) * D.T @ np.kron(Si, Si) @ D
    return I

def fisher_obs(Sig, R):
    p = Sig.shape[0]; kk = p + p * (p + 1) // 2
    vi = vech_index_table(p); I = np.zeros((kk, kk))
    pats = {}
    for r in range(R.shape[0]):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats[key] = pats.get(key, 0) + 1
    for key, npat in pats.items():
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]; q = len(Oi)
        if q == 0:
            continue
        Sooi = inv(Sig[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)])
        for a in range(q):
            for b in range(q):
                I[Oi[a], Oi[b]] += npat * Sooi[a, b]
        Dq = duplication_matrix(q)
        Isub = 0.5 * Dq.T @ np.kron(Sooi, Sooi) @ Dq
        full = np.array([p + vi[Oi[a], Oi[b]] - 1
                         for b in range(q) for a in range(b, q)])
        I[np.ix_(full, full)] += npat * Isub
    return I

def loglik(mu, Sig, X):
    Si = inv(Sig); _, ld = slogdet(Sig); hl = 0.5 * np.log(2 * np.pi); p = len(mu)
    d = X - mu
    return float(np.sum(-p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * np.einsum('ij,jk,ik->i', d, Si, d)))

def barQ_fiml(mu_e, Sig_e, mu_i, Sig_i, Y, R):
    Np, p = Y.shape
    Sei = inv(Sig_e); _, ld = slogdet(Sig_e); hl = 0.5 * np.log(2 * np.pi)
    total = 0.0; pats = {}
    for r in range(Np):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats.setdefault(key, []).append(r)
    for key, rows in pats.items():
        Mi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 1]
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]
        if not Mi:                              # fully observed
            for r in rows:
                d = Y[r] - mu_e
                total += -p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d
        elif not Oi:                            # all missing
            d = mu_i - mu_e; tr = np.trace(Sei @ Sig_i)
            total += len(rows) * (-p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d - 0.5 * tr)
        else:                                   # partial: Schur conditional
            Sooi = inv(Sig_i[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)]); Smo = Sig_i[np.ix_(Mi, Oi)]
            condV = Sig_i[np.ix_(Mi, Mi)] - Smo @ Sooi @ Smo.T
            tr = np.trace(Sei[np.ix_(Mi, Mi)] @ condV)
            for r in rows:
                cm = mu_i[Mi] + Smo @ Sooi @ (Y[r, Oi] - mu_i[Oi])
                Xh = np.zeros(p)
                for o in Oi:
                    Xh[o] = Y[r, o]
                for jj, mm in enumerate(Mi):
                    Xh[mm] = cm[jj]
                d = Xh - mu_e
                total += -p * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * d @ Sei @ d - 0.5 * tr
    return total
```

The fixture `proof-verification-fixture.json` is one W1 replicate (N=500, non-monotone
MAR) plus the `W1-sweep-prod` cell aggregates, emitted by `verification/W1-sympy-fixture.R`.

```{python}
cands = [Path("proof-verification-fixture.json"),
         Path("manuscript/proof-verification-fixture.json")]
fix = next(p for p in cands if p.exists())
J = json.load(open(fix))

def as_float(x):
    try:
        return float(x)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        return np.nan       # jsonlite serializes missing entries of Y as "NA"

p, k, N = J["meta"]["p"], J["meta"]["k"], J["meta"]["N"]
X = np.array(J["data"]["X"], float)
Y = np.array([[as_float(v) for v in row] for row in J["data"]["Y"]])
R = np.array(J["data"]["R"], float)
mu_obs = np.array(J["theta_obs"]["mu"], float)
Sig_obs = np.array(J["theta_obs"]["Sigma"], float)
mu_com = np.array(J["theta_com"]["mu"], float)
Sig_com = np.array(J["theta_com"]["Sigma"], float)
I_com_R = np.array(J["fisher"]["I_com"], float)
I_obs_R = np.array(J["fisher"]["I_obs"], float)
sc = J["scalars"]; cache = J["cache"]
print(f"replicate: p={p} k={k} N={N}, missing entries={int(np.isnan(Y).sum())}")
```

**Fisher information and tr(RIV) match R** (D15, @eq-trriv):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-fisher
#| lst-cap: "MVN I_com, I_obs, tr(RIV) match 00-setup.R to machine precision (D15)."
I_com_py = fisher_com(mu_obs, Sig_obs, N)
I_obs_py = fisher_obs(Sig_obs, R)
trRIV_py = float(np.trace(inv(I_obs_py) @ I_com_py) - k)

err_com = np.max(np.abs(I_com_py - I_com_R))
err_obs = np.max(np.abs(I_obs_py - I_obs_R))
print(f"max|I_com_py - I_com_R| = {err_com:.2e}")
print(f"max|I_obs_py - I_obs_R| = {err_obs:.2e}")
print(f"tr(RIV) py = {trRIV_py:.12f}  | R = {sc['trRIV_replicate']:.12f}"
      f"  | diff = {trRIV_py - sc['trRIV_replicate']:.2e}")
assert err_com < 1e-8 and err_obs < 1e-8
assert abs(trRIV_py - sc["trRIV_replicate"]) < 1e-8
```

**Information identity and $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$** (D7, @eq-info-identity):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-psd
#| lst-cap: "Information identity holds and I_mis|obs is PSD at the fixture (D7)."
I_mis_py = I_com_py - I_obs_py
print("max|I_com - (I_obs + I_mis)| =",
      f"{np.max(np.abs(I_com_py - (I_obs_py + I_mis_py))):.2e}")
print("min eigenvalue of I_mis|obs  =", f"{eigvalsh(I_mis_py).min():.4e}  (>= 0)")
assert eigvalsh(I_mis_py).min() > -1e-8
```

**FIML Q-function and the A / B / T decomposition match R** (D9, D10-D12):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-fiml
#| lst-cap: "Analytic FIML Q-function and A/B/T match 00-setup.R (D9-D12)."
barL_py = barQ_fiml(mu_obs, Sig_obs, mu_obs, Sig_obs, Y, R)
ell_com_at_com_py = loglik(mu_com, Sig_com, X)
ell_com_at_obs_py = loglik(mu_obs, Sig_obs, X)
A_py = barL_py - ell_com_at_obs_py
B_py = ell_com_at_obs_py - ell_com_at_com_py
T_py = barL_py - ell_com_at_com_py

for name, py, r in [("barL_obs", barL_py, sc["barL_obs"]),
                    ("A_r", A_py, sc["A_r"]),
                    ("B_r", B_py, sc["B_r"]),
                    ("T_r", T_py, sc["T_r"])]:
    print(f"{name:9s} py = {py:14.8f}  | R = {r:14.8f}  | diff = {py - r:.2e}")
    assert abs(py - r) < 1e-6
```

**$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ within Monte-Carlo SE of $\mathbb{E}[T]$**
(@eq-theorem against the W1 production cache):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-mcse
#| lst-cap: "1/2 tr(RIV) sits within the MCSE band of the W1 cache E[T]."
half_pred_rep = 0.5 * trRIV_py            # from this replicate's theta_obs
half_pred_mc = cache["half_trRIV_pred"]   # 0.5 * MC-mean of analytic tr(RIV)
T_hat, T_mcse = cache["T_hat"], cache["T_mcse"]
lo, hi = T_hat - 1.96 * T_mcse, T_hat + 1.96 * T_mcse

print(f"cache E[T]            = {T_hat:.4f}  +/- {T_mcse:.4f} (MCSE)")
print(f"95% MCSE band         = [{lo:.4f}, {hi:.4f}]")
print(f"1/2 tr(RIV) [MC mean] = {half_pred_mc:.4f}")
print(f"1/2 tr(RIV) [replicate] = {half_pred_rep:.4f}")
print(f"cache E[A] = {cache['A_hat']:.3f} +/- {cache['A_mcse']:.3f}"
      f"  vs tr(RIV)_MC = {cache['trRIV_mean']:.3f}")
print(f"cache E[B] = {cache['B_hat']:.3f} +/- {cache['B_mcse']:.3f}"
      f"  vs -1/2 tr(RIV)_MC = {-half_pred_mc:.3f}")
assert lo <= half_pred_mc <= hi
print("\nGATE PASSED: 1/2 tr(RIV) lies within the MCSE band of E[T].")
```

### Term A mechanism: the conditional-variance penalty {#sec-verify-termA-mech}

The $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ of Term A (@sec-termA) enters the FIML Q-function
through the per-observation conditional-variance penalty
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma_{\text{eval}}^{-1}\bar V_i)$, where for a partial
pattern $V = \Sigma_{MM} - \Sigma_{MO}\Sigma_{OO}^{-1}\Sigma_{OM}$ is the Schur
complement (@sec-alg). Shown symbolically for a 3-variable pattern, $X_1$ missing:

```{python}
syms = sp.symbols('s11 s12 s13 s22 s23 s33', positive=True)
s11, s12, s13, s22, s23, s33 = syms
Sig3 = sp.Matrix([[s11, s12, s13], [s12, s22, s23], [s13, s23, s33]])
Mi, Oi = [0], [1, 2]
Smm = Sig3[Mi, Mi]; Smo = Sig3[Mi, Oi]; Soo = Sig3[Oi, Oi]
V = sp.simplify(Smm - Smo * Soo.inv() * Smo.T)     # Schur complement, scalar here
print("conditional Var(X_1 | X_2, X_3) =")
sp.pprint(V[0, 0])
```

The pattern-sum-to-$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ reduction is a conditional-expectation
calculation Sympy does not carry out cleanly; it is verified numerically (`A_py` matches R
to machine precision in @lst-mc-fiml, and the cache confirms $\mathbb{E}[A]\approx\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$).

### Observed information under MAR: the cross term (Appendix C) {#sec-verify-crossterm}

Appendix C claims the observed information carries a mean $\times$ (co)variance **cross block**
under MAR that the naive block-diagonal form omits, and that it is the selection-induced effect
of @kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998. Both halves are checked inline here. First the
observed information in closed form (the realized negative Hessian, with per-pattern residual
$r_P$ and cross-product $M_P$), validated against a finite-difference Hessian of
$\ell_{\text{obs}}$ on the fixture replicate:

```{python}
def loglik_obs(mu, Sig, Y, R):
    p = len(mu); hl = 0.5 * np.log(2 * np.pi); total = 0.0; pats = {}
    for r in range(Y.shape[0]):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats.setdefault(key, []).append(r)
    for key, rows in pats.items():
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]; q = len(Oi)
        if q == 0:
            continue
        S = Sig[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)]; Si = inv(S); _, ld = slogdet(S)
        for r in rows:
            d = Y[np.ix_([r], Oi)][0] - mu[Oi]
            total += -q * hl - 0.5 * ld - 0.5 * (d @ Si @ d)
    return total

def observed_info_obs(mu, Sig, Y, R):
    # Realized negative Hessian of l_obs -- the OBSERVED information (mirrors
    # observed_info_obs_mvn in 00-setup.R): mean block n_P S^-1; mean x vech
    # (r_P' S^-1 (x) S^-1) D_q (propto r_P); vech x vech with M_P.
    p = len(mu); kk = p + p * (p + 1) // 2; vi = vech_index_table(p)
    J = np.zeros((kk, kk)); pats = {}
    for r in range(Y.shape[0]):
        key = tuple(R[r].astype(int)); pats.setdefault(key, []).append(r)
    for key, rows in pats.items():
        Oi = [i for i in range(p) if key[i] == 0]; q = len(Oi)
        if q == 0:
            continue
        S = Sig[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)]; Si = inv(S); nP = len(rows)
        d = Y[np.ix_(rows, Oi)] - mu[Oi]                       # nP x q residuals
        rP = d.sum(axis=0); MP = d.T @ d                        # q ; q x q
        Dq = duplication_matrix(q)
        full = np.array([p + vi[Oi[a], Oi[b]] - 1
                         for b in range(q) for a in range(b, q)])
        J[np.ix_(Oi, Oi)] += nP * Si                            # mean block
        cross = np.kron((rP @ Si)[None, :], Si) @ Dq            # q x |vech|
        J[np.ix_(Oi, full)] += cross
        J[np.ix_(full, Oi)] += cross.T
        SiMSi = Si @ MP @ Si
        vv = Dq.T @ (-0.5 * nP * np.kron(Si, Si)
                     + 0.5 * (np.kron(SiMSi, Si) + np.kron(Si, SiMSi))) @ Dq
        J[np.ix_(full, full)] += vv
    return J
```

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-obsinfo
#| lst-cap: "Observed information = realized -Hessian of l_obs; carries a cross block the naive form omits; RIV is larger (Appendix C)."
def pack(mu, Sig):
    return np.concatenate([mu, [Sig[i, j] for j in range(p) for i in range(j, p)]])
def unpack(th):
    mu = th[:p]; Sig = np.zeros((p, p)); c = p
    for j in range(p):
        for i in range(j, p):
            Sig[i, j] = Sig[j, i] = th[c]; c += 1
    return mu, Sig
th0 = pack(mu_obs, Sig_obs); hh = 1e-4
def fobs(th):
    mu_, Sig_ = unpack(th); return loglik_obs(mu_, Sig_, Y, R)
Hfd = np.zeros((k, k))
for a in range(k):
    for b in range(a, k):
        ea = np.zeros(k); ea[a] = hh; eb = np.zeros(k); eb[b] = hh
        Hfd[a, b] = Hfd[b, a] = (fobs(th0 + ea + eb) - fobs(th0 + ea - eb)
                                 - fobs(th0 - ea + eb) + fobs(th0 - ea - eb)) / (4 * hh * hh)
O_obs_py = observed_info_obs(mu_obs, Sig_obs, Y, R)
rel_fd = np.max(np.abs(O_obs_py - (-Hfd))) / np.max(np.abs(O_obs_py))
cross_obs = np.max(np.abs(O_obs_py[:p, p:]))            # observed: mean x vech block
cross_naive = np.max(np.abs(I_obs_py[:p, p:]))          # naive fisher_obs: block-diagonal
scale = np.max(np.abs(np.diag(O_obs_py)))
trRIV_obs = float(np.trace(inv(O_obs_py) @ I_com_py) - k)
print(f"observed info vs -Hessian(l_obs): max rel diff = {rel_fd:.2e}")
print(f"cross block  observed = {cross_obs:8.3f} ({cross_obs/scale:.3f} of diag)"
      f"  |  naive = {cross_naive:.2e}")
print(f"tr(RIV)  observed = {trRIV_obs:.4f}  |  naive = {trRIV_py:.4f}")
assert rel_fd < 1e-3                                    # observed info IS the realized Hessian
assert cross_naive < 1e-9                               # naive form is block-diagonal
assert cross_obs > 0.01 * scale                         # observed form has a real cross block
```

The cross block is non-zero because, under MAR, selection on the observed values shifts each
pattern's observed mean off the marginal, so $\mathbb{E}[r_P]\neq0$. The selected-Gaussian closed
form (Appendix C) — non-zero under MAR, exactly zero under MCAR — against a seeded simulation:

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-crossterm
#| lst-cap: "Selection-induced mean shift E[r_P] matches the inverse-Mills closed form (MAR); vanishes under MCAR."
import math
rng = np.random.default_rng(20260531)
mu0 = np.zeros(3)
Sig0 = np.array([[1.0, 0.3, 0.5], [0.3, 1.0, 0.2], [0.5, 0.2, 1.0]])
a_sel, b_sel, Nbig = -0.5, 0.4, 2_000_000
Xs = rng.multivariate_normal(mu0, Sig0, size=Nbig)
def Phi(z): return 0.5 * (1.0 + math.erf(z / math.sqrt(2.0)))
def phi(z): return math.exp(-0.5 * z * z) / math.sqrt(2.0 * math.pi)
for label, b in [("MAR ", b_sel), ("MCAR", 0.0)]:
    V = rng.standard_normal(Nbig)                       # R=1 (coord 0 missing) iff V - b*X[:,2] < a
    sel = (V - b * Xs[:, 2]) < a_sel
    zc = a_sel / math.sqrt(1 + b * b); lam = phi(zc) / Phi(zc)
    pred = (b * Sig0[:, 2] / math.sqrt(1 + b * b)) * lam       # E[X_k - mu_k | selected]
    emp = Xs[sel].mean(axis=0) - mu0
    print(f"{label}: shift empirical = {np.round(emp, 4)}  | closed form = {np.round(pred, 4)}")
    if b == 0.0:
        assert np.max(np.abs(emp)) < 5e-3                      # MCAR: no selection shift
    else:
        assert np.max(np.abs(emp - pred)) < 5e-3              # MAR: matches inverse-Mills form
```

### Tier B — abstract generalization (MVN-free) {#sec-verify-tierB}

The trace bookkeeping above used abstract `MatrixSymbol` objects with no MVN commitment,
so the **leading-order** $T = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ holds for any
regular likelihood given L1-L4 **and an estimated scale** — the assumption behind Term A's
$\operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}V_{\text{obs}})$ input, which needs the entropy-gradient
cancellation of @sec-termA and so requires $\alpha\neq0$. For a known-scale, location-only fit that
input halves and $T=0$ (scope remark after the theorem). Under MAR, add the design-imbalance $(A)+(C)$
of @sec-termA, which is $0$ under MCAR. What remains MVN-free to verify is that the score-equation expansion delivers
the covariance input L2 (@eq-varGap): the observed score is the conditional expectation of
the complete-data score under MAR, so $\mathbb{E}[U_{\text{com}}U_{\text{obs}}^\top]=I_{\text{obs}}$.

```{python}
# (L2) from the efficient-estimator orthogonality, symbolically.
Vobs = I_obs.inv()                                  # Var(theta_obs)        (L1)
Vcom = I_com.inv()                                  # Var(theta_com)
Cov_oc = (I_obs.inv() * I_obs * I_com.inv()).doit() # Cov(obs,com) = I_com^{-1}
print("Cov(theta_obs, theta_com) =", Cov_oc, " (= I_com^{-1} = Var(theta_com))")
Vdiff = symmetric_reduce(sp.MatAdd(Vobs, -Cov_oc, -Cov_oc.T, Vcom).doit())
target = (I_obs.inv() - I_com.inv()).doit()
print("Var(diff) - (I_obs^{-1} - I_com^{-1}) =", sp.expand(symmetric_reduce(Vdiff - target)))
```

Feeding the symbolic L2 straight back into Term B and recombining — no separately-asserted
covariance, a pure consequence of the score relations (L2 @eq-varGap):

```{python}
#| label: lst-mc-tierb
#| lst-cap: "Tier B: L2 derived from the score relations recombines to tr(RIV)/2 (MVN-free)."
B_from_scores = trace_normal(sp.Rational(-1, 2) * Trace(I_com * Vdiff), n)
A_from_L1 = trace_normal(Trace(I_mis * Vobs), n)
T_B = trace_normal(A_from_L1, n) + B_from_scores
assert sp.expand(T_B - trRIV / 2) == 0
print("Tier B:  T = A + B =", sp.expand(T_B), " == tr(RIV)/2  [exact]")
```

### Verification summary {#sec-verify-summary}

| Identity | Status | Listing |
|---|---|---|
| $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) = \operatorname{tr}(I_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{com}}) - k$ | symbolic | @lst-mc-trriv |
| Term A $= +\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (given L1, L3) | symbolic | @lst-mc-terma |
| Term B $= -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (given L2, L4) | symbolic | @lst-mc-termb |
| $T = A + B = +\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ | symbolic | @lst-mc-combine |
| MVN $I_{\text{com}}, I_{\text{obs}}, \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ | numeric (machine precision) | @lst-mc-fisher |
| Information identity, $I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}}\succeq 0$ | numeric | @lst-mc-psd |
| FIML Q-function, $A/B/T$ | numeric (machine precision) | @lst-mc-fiml |
| $\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ vs $\mathbb{E}[T]$ | within MCSE | @lst-mc-mcse |
| Observed info $=$ realized $-$Hessian; cross block (naive omits); larger RIV | numeric (FD, MAR fixture) | @lst-mc-obsinfo |
| MAR cross term $\mathbb{E}[r_P]$ vs inverse-Mills closed form; MCAR $=0$ | numeric (seeded sim) | @lst-mc-crossterm |
| Tier B (abstract, MVN-free): L2 from scores | symbolic | @lst-mc-tierb |

---

## Appendix C — The observed-data information under MAR {#sec-appendix-c}

The RIV's denominator is the observed-data information $I_{\text{obs}}$ (@sec-riv), which
under MAR is *not* the block-diagonal naive form: the unconditional expectation carries a
mean–covariance cross term [@kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §3.2], <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:374</code></a>,
and is estimated by the observed information $-\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}/\partial\theta\partial\theta^\top$.
This appendix gives that observed information in closed form for the MVN under missingness — so the
RIV is computable analytically, with no imputations and no numerical differentiation — and shows
exactly where the cross term comes from.

**Observed information.** The observed information is the realized negative Hessian
$\mathcal{J}_{\text{obs}}(\theta;Y)=-\partial^2\ell_{\text{obs}}/\partial\theta\,\partial\theta^\top$,
the quantity whose expectation is $I_{\text{obs}}$ (D6). It is the second differential
$-d^2\ell$ *before* the expectation that yields the Fisher information
[@magnusMatrixDifferentialCalculus2019, Ch 15 §3, §6], <a href="#sec-src-magnus"><code>:1800</code></a>;
the component derivatives (of $\log|S|$ and of the quadratic form) are
[@petersen2008matrix, §2.4.4], <a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:510</code></a>, the Gaussian
second derivative carrying the residual structure $S^{-1}ee^\top S^{-1}-S^{-1}$
[<a href="#sec-src-petersen"><code>:2013</code></a>].

Per missingness pattern $P$ (observed set $O$, $n_P$ observations, $S=\Sigma_{OO}$,
$q=|O|$, $D_q$ the duplication matrix), with residual sum $r_P=\sum_{i\in P}(y_i^O-\mu_O)$ and
cross-product $M_P=\sum_{i\in P}(y_i^O-\mu_O)(y_i^O-\mu_O)^\top$, the pattern's contribution to
$\mathcal{J}_{\text{obs}}$ in $(\mu,\operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$ coordinates is, blockwise,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mean}:\quad & n_P\,S^{-1} \quad(\text{data-free; equals the expected-info block}),\\
\text{mean}\times\operatorname{vech}:\quad & (r_P^\top S^{-1}\otimes S^{-1})\,D_q \quad(\propto r_P),\\
\operatorname{vech}\times\operatorname{vech}:\quad & D_q^\top\!\Big[-\tfrac{n_P}{2}(S^{-1}\!\otimes S^{-1})+\tfrac12\big((S^{-1}M_PS^{-1}\otimes S^{-1})+(S^{-1}\otimes S^{-1}M_PS^{-1})\big)\Big]D_q,
\end{aligned}
$$
summed over patterns and embedded in the full index set exactly as in `fisher_info_obs_mvn`.
Taking expectations, $\mathbb{E}[M_P]=n_PS$ collapses the $\operatorname{vech}$ block to
$\tfrac{n_P}{2}D_q^\top(S^{-1}\!\otimes S^{-1})D_q$, and the cross block is governed by
$\mathbb{E}[r_P]$. Setting $\mathbb{E}[r_P]=0$ zeroes it and recovers the block-diagonal
$I_{\text{obs}}$ of D15 (`fisher_info_obs_mvn`) — but **$\mathbb{E}[r_P]=0$ holds only under MCAR.**
`[mc]` the observed information equals the realized $-$Hessian of $\ell_{\text{obs}}$ (finite
differences, @lst-mc-obsinfo here; numerical Hessian in `verification/00-test-primitives.R`), it
carries a cross block the naive form omits, and its RIV is the larger one.

**The cross term under MAR.** Under MAR the missingness depends on the observed values, so each
pattern's observed mean is selected away from the marginal and $\mathbb{E}[r_P]\neq 0$. For the
running example's mechanism — coordinate $j$ made missing with probability $\Phi(a+b\,y_{\text{sel}})$,
equivalently selection on a Gaussian $S_{\text{sel}}=V-b\,y_{\text{sel}}$ with $V\sim N(0,1)$
independent — the selected-Gaussian moment gives, per selected coordinate,
$$ \mathbb{E}[\,y_k-\mu_k \mid \text{selected}\,] \;=\; \frac{b\,\Sigma_{k,\text{sel}}}{\sqrt{1+b^2}}\;
\lambda\!\Big(\tfrac{a}{\sqrt{1+b^2}}\Big), \qquad \lambda(z)=\phi(z)/\Phi(z) $$
(the inverse Mills ratio). The shift is $\propto b\,(\Sigma\text{-coupling})$ — non-zero under MAR,
exactly zero under MCAR ($b=0$) — so $\mathbb{E}[r_P]\neq0$ and the cross block
$(\mathbb{E}[r_P]^\top S^{-1}\!\otimes S^{-1})D_q$ survives in the unconditional $I_{\text{obs}}$. This
is the MVN, probit-mechanism instance of @kenwardLikelihoodBasedFrequentist1998, §3.2, whose
logistic-mechanism bivariate version has the same structure
$\mathbb{E}[y_2-\mu_2\mid r]=\beta\alpha_1$, $\beta=\sigma_{12}\sigma_{11}^{-1}$, <a href="#sec-src-kenward"><code>:374</code></a>.
`[mc]` the closed-form shift matches a seeded simulation under MAR and vanishes under MCAR
(@lst-mc-crossterm here; the project mechanism against $4\times10^6$ draws, MAR shift
$\approx[0.124,0,0.414,0.207]$, in `todo/008`); the cross-block contribution to
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ does not decay with $n$ (`todo/008`).

**The RIV and the IC correction.** The (single) RIV is
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathcal{J}_{\text{obs}}^{-1}I_{\text{mis}\mid\text{obs}})$ — the observed
information $\mathcal{J}_{\text{obs}}$ above in place of the naive block-diagonal $I_{\text{obs}}$,
the quantity `tr_riv_observed_general` computes. The information-criterion correction (manuscript
§5) subtracts this per candidate model. For model *selection* specifically, using the naive
block-diagonal information instead is, empirically, close to a wash: the naive-vs-unconditional gap
is roughly proportional across candidate models, so it nearly cancels in the $\arg\min$
(`w3-sweep.R`); the residual gap to the oracle is finite-sample MI-AIC noise. The correction is not
optional, however, for the bias *level* — the deviance-bias estimate itself and likelihood-ratio
test calibration — where the cross term must be carried.

---

### References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
